105

Proposition 5.6.3 All the properties in Propositions 4.3.10 and 4.3.12 remain valid

if x and y are assumed to be real numbers instead of rational numbers.

Instead of giving an actual proof of this proposition, we shall give a meta-proof

(an argument appealing to the nature of proofs, rather than the nature of real and

rational numbers).

Meta-proof. If one inspects the proof of Propositions 4.3.10 and 4.3.12 we see that

they rely on the laws of algebra and the laws of order for the rationals (Propositions

4.2.4 and 4.2.9). But by Propositions 5.3.11 and 5.4.7, and the identity xx−1 =

x−1x = 1 we know that all these laws of algebra and order continue to hold for real

numbers as well as rationals. Thus we can modify the proof of Proposition 4.3.10

and 4.3.12 to hold in the case when x and y are real.

□

Now we consider exponentiation to exponents which are not integers. We begin

with the notion of an nth root, which we can define using our notion of supremum.

Definition 5.6.4 Let x \geq0 be a non-negative real, and let n \geq1 be a positive integer.

We define x1/n, also known as the nth root of x, by the formula

x1/n := sup{y \inR : y \geq0 and yn \leqx}.

We often write √x for x1/2.

Note we do not define the nth root of a negative number. In fact, we will leave the

nth roots of negative numbers undefined for the rest of the text (one can define these

nth roots once one defines the complex numbers, but we shall refrain from doing so).

Lemma 5.6.5 (Existence of nth roots). Let x \geq0 be a non-negative real, and let

n \geq1 be a positive integer. Then the set E := {y \inR : y \geq0 and yn \leqx} is nonempty and is also bounded above. In particular, x1/n is a real number.

Proof The set E contains 0 (why?), so it is certainly not empty. Now we show it has

an upper bound. We divide into two cases: x \leq1 and x > 1. First suppose that we

are in the case where x \leq1. Then we claim that the set E is bounded above by 1.

To see this, suppose for sake of contradiction that there was an element y \inE for

which y > 1. But then yn > 1 (why?), and hence yn > x, a contradiction. Thus E

has an upper bound. Now suppose that we are in the case where x > 1. Then we

claim that the set E is bounded above by x. To see this, suppose for contradiction

that there was an element y \inE for which y > x. Since x > 1, we thus have y > 1.

Since y > x and y > 1, we have yn > x (why?), a contradiction. Thus in both cases

E has an upper bound, and so x1/n is finite.

□

We list some basic properties of nth roots below.

Lemma 5.6.6 Let x, y \geq0 be non-negative reals, and let n, m \geq1 be positive integers.

106

The Real Numbers

(a) If y = x1/n, then yn = x.

(b) Conversely, if yn = x, then y = x1/n.

(c) x1/n is a non-negative real number, and is positive iff x is positive.

(d) We have x > y if and only if x1/n > y1/n.

(e) If x > 1, then x1/k is a decreasing function of k, where k ranges over the positive

integers; that is to say, x1/k < x1/l whenever k > l. If 0 < x < 1, then x1/k is

an increasing function of k (i.e., x1/k > x1/l whenever k > l). If x = 1, then

x1/k = 1 for all k.

(f ) We have (xy)1/n = x1/ny1/n.

(g) We have (x1/n)1/m = x1/nm.

Proof See Exercise 5.6.1.

□

The observant reader may note that this definition of x1/n might possibly be

inconsistent with our previous notion of xn when n = 1, but it is easy to check that

x1/1 = x = x1 (why?), so there is no inconsistency.

One consequence of Lemma 5.6.6(b) is another proof of the cancelation law from

Proposition 4.3.12(c) and Proposition 5.6.3: if y and z are positive and yn = zn, then

y = z. (Why does this follow from Lemma 5.6.6(b)?) Note that this only works when

y and z are positive; for instance, (−3)2 = 32, but we cannot conclude from this that

−3 = 3.

Now we define how to raise a positive number x to a rational exponent q.

Definition 5.6.7 Let x > 0 be a positive real number, and let q be a rational number.

To define xq, we write q = a/b for some integer a and positive integer b, and define

xq := (x1/b)a.

Note that every rational q, whether positive, negative, or zero, can be written in

the form a/b where a is an integer and b is positive (why?). However, the rational

number q can be expressed in the form a/b in more than one way, for instance 1/2

can also be expressed as 2/4 or 3/6. So to ensure that this definition is well-defined,

we need to check that different expressions a/b give the same formula for xq:

Lemma 5.6.8 Let a, a′ be integers and b, b′ be positive integers such that a/b =

a′/b′, and let x be a positive real number. Then we have (x1/b′)a′ = (x1/b)a.

Proof There are three cases: a = 0, a > 0, a < 0. If a = 0, then we must have

a′ = 0 (why?) and so both (x1/b′)a′ and (x1/b)a are equal to 1, so we are done.

Nowsupposethata > 0.Thena′ > 0(why?),andab′ = ba′.Write y := x1/(ab′) =

x1/(ba′). By Lemma 5.6.6(g) we have y = (x1/b′)1/a and y = (x1/b)1/a′; by Lemma

5.6.6(a) we thus have ya = x1/b′ and ya′ = x1/b. Thus we have

(x1/b′)a′ = (ya)a′ = yaa′ = (ya′)a = (x1/b)a

as desired.

107

Finally,supposethata < 0.Thenwehave(−a)/b = (−a′)/b′.But−a ispositive,

so the previous case applies and we have (x1/b′)−a′ = (x1/b)−a. Taking the reciprocal

of both sides we obtain the result.

□

Thus xq is well-defined for every rational q. Note that this new definition is

consistent with our old definition for x1/n (why?) and is also consistent with our old

definition for xn (why?).

Some basic facts about rational exponentiation:

Lemma 5.6.9 Let x, y > 0 be positive reals, and let q,r be rationals.

(a) xq is a positive real.

(b) xq+r = xqxr and (xq)r = xqr.

(c) x−q = 1/xq.

(d) If q > 0, then x > y if and only if xq > yq.

(e) If x > 1, then xq > xr if and only if q > r. If x < 1, then xq > xr if and only if

q < r.

(f ) (xy)q = xq yq.

Proof See Exercise 5.6.2.

□

We still have to do real exponentiation; in other words, we still have to define x y

where x > 0 and y is a real number—but we will defer that until Sect. 6.7, once we

have formalized the concept of limit.

In the rest of the text we shall now just assume the real numbers to obey all the

usual laws of algebra, order, and exponentiation.

Exercise 5.6.1 Prove Lemma 5.6.6. (Hints: review the proof of Proposition 5.5.12. Also, you will

find proof by contradiction a useful tool, especially when combined with the trichotomy of order

in Proposition 5.4.7 and Proposition 5.4.12. The earlier parts of the lemma can be used to prove

later parts of the lemma. With part (e), first show that if x > 1 then x1/n > 1, and if x < 1 then

x1/n < 1.)

Exercise 5.6.2 Prove Lemma 5.6.9. (Hint: you should rely mainly on Lemma 5.6.6 and on algebra.)

Exercise 5.6.3 If x is a real number and n is an even natural number (thus n = 2m for some natural

number m), show that xn \geq0.

Exercise 5.6.4 If x is a real number, show that |x| = (x2)1/2.

Exercise 5.6.5 If x, y are positive reals, and q is a positive rational with q \geq1, show that

max(xq, yq) = max(x, y)q and min(xq, yq) = min(x, y)q, where the operations min, max were

defined in Exercise 5.4.9. What happens if we have q < 1 instead of q \geq1?

105

命题 5.6.3 命题 4.3.10 和 4.3.12 中的所有性质在假设 x 和 y 为实数而非有理数时仍然成立。

下面我们给出一个元证明（一个诉诸证明本质的论证，而非诉诸实数与有理数本质的论证），而不是给出该命题的实际证明。

元证明：如果考察命题 4.3.10 和 4.3.12 的证明，我们发现它们依赖于有理数的代数定律和次序定律（命题 4.2.4 和 4.2.9）。但根据命题 5.3.11 和 5.4.7，以及恒等式 $xx^{-1} = x^{-1}x = 1$，我们知道所有这些代数定律和次序定律对实数同样成立。因此，我们可以修改命题 4.3.10 和 4.3.12 的证明，使其在 x 和 y 为实数的情况下也成立。

□

现在我们考虑指数为非整数的指数运算。我们从 n 次根的概念开始，可以使用上确界的概念来定义。

定义 5.6.4 设 $x \geq 0$ 为非负实数，$n \geq 1$ 为正整数。我们将 $x^{1/n}$（也称为 x 的 n 次根）定义为

$$x^{1/n} := \sup\{y \in \mathbb{R} : y \geq 0 \text{ 且 } y^n \leq x\}$$

我们通常将 $\sqrt{x}$ 写成 $x^{1/2}$。

注意我们不定义负数的 n 次根。事实上，在本文的其余部分中，我们将使负数的 n 次根保持未定义状态（一旦定义了复数，就可以定义这些 n 次根，但我们将避免这样做）。

引理 5.6.5（n 次根的存在性）。设 $x \geq 0$ 为非负实数，$n \geq 1$ 为正整数。则集合 $E := \{y \in \mathbb{R} : y \geq 0 \text{ 且 } y^n \leq x\}$ 非空且有上界。特别是，$x^{1/n}$ 是一个实数。

证明 集合 E 包含 0（为什么？），所以它肯定非空。现在我们证明它有上界。我们分两种情况讨论：$x \leq 1$ 和 $x > 1$。首先假设在 $x \leq 1$ 的情况下。那么我们断言集合 E 被 1 所上界。为了看出这一点，假设为了矛盾，存在一个元素 $y \in E$ 使得 $y > 1$。但这样 $y^n > 1$（为什么？），从而 $y^n > x$，矛盾。因此 E 有上界。现在假设在 $x > 1$ 的情况下。那么我们断言集合 E 被 x 所上界。为了看出这一点，假设矛盾地存在一个元素 $y \in E$ 使得 $y > x$。由于 $x > 1$，我们有 $y > 1$。由于 $y > x$ 且 $y > 1$，我们有 $y^n > x$（为什么？），矛盾。因此在这两种情况下 E 都有上界，所以 $x^{1/n}$ 是有限的。

□

下面我们列出 n 次根的一些基本性质。

引理 5.6.6 设 $x, y \geq 0$ 为非负实数，$n, m \geq 1$ 为正整数。

106

实数

(a) 若 $y = x^{1/n}$，则 $y^n = x$。

(b) 反过来，若 $y^n = x$，则 $y = x^{1/n}$。

(c) $x^{1/n}$ 是非负实数，且当且仅当 x 为正数时为正数。

(d) 我们有 $x > y$ 当且仅当 $x^{1/n} > y^{1/n}$。

(e) 若 $x > 1$，则 $x^{1/k}$ 是 k 的递减函数，k 取正整数；即当 $k > l$ 时 $x^{1/k} < x^{1/l}$。若 $0 < x < 1$，则 $x^{1/k}$ 是 k 的递增函数（即当 $k > l$ 时 $x^{1/k} > x^{1/l}$）。若 $x = 1$，则对所有 k 有 $x^{1/k} = 1$。

(f) 我们有 $(xy)^{1/n} = x^{1/n} y^{1/n}$。

(g) 我们有 $(x^{1/n})^{1/m} = x^{1/(nm)}$。

证明 参见习题 5.6.1。

□

细心的读者可能会注意到，$x^{1/n}$ 的这个定义可能与之前 n = 1 时 x^n 的定义不一致，但容易验证 $x^{1/1} = x = x^1$（为什么？），因此没有不一致。

引理 5.6.6(b) 的一个推论是命题 4.3.12(c) 和命题 5.6.3 中消去律的另一个证明：如果 y 和 z 为正数且 $y^n = z^n$，则 $y = z$。（为什么这可以从引理 5.6.6(b) 得出？）注意这仅在 y 和 z 为正数时有效；例如，$(-3)^2 = 3^2$，但我们不能由此推出 $-3 = 3$。

现在我们定义如何将正数 x 提升到有理指数 q。

定义 5.6.7 设 $x > 0$ 为正实数，q 为有理数。为了定义 $x^q$，我们将 q 写成 $a/b$ 的形式，其中 a 为整数，b 为正整数，定义为

$$x^q := (x^{1/b})^a$$

注意每个有理数 q，无论是正数、负数还是零，都可以写成 a/b 的形式，其中 a 为整数，b 为正数（为什么？）。然而，有理数 q 可以用多于一种方式表示为 a/b，例如 1/2 也可以表示为 2/4 或 3/6。因此，为了确保这个定义是良定义的，我们需要验证不同的 a/b 表达式给出相同的 $x^q$ 公式：

引理 5.6.8 设 a, a' 为整数，b, b' 为正整数使得 $a/b = a'/b'$，设 x 为正实数。则我们有 $(x^{1/b'})^{a'} = (x^{1/b})^a$。

证明 有三种情况：$a = 0$，$a > 0$，$a < 0$。若 $a = 0$，则必有 $a' = 0$（为什么？），于是 $(x^{1/b'})^{a'}$ 和 $(x^{1/b})^a$ 都等于 1，证毕。

现在假设 $a > 0$。则 $a' > 0$（为什么？），且 $ab' = ba'$。记 $y := x^{1/(ab')} = x^{1/(ba')}$。由引理 5.6.6(g) 我们有 $y = (x^{1/b'})^{1/a}$ 且 $y = (x^{1/b})^{1/a'}$；由引理 5.6.6(a) 我们有 $y^a = x^{1/b'}$ 且 $y^{a'} = x^{1/b}$。于是我们有

$$(x^{1/b'})^{a'} = (y^a)^{a'} = y^{aa'} = (y^{a'})^a = (x^{1/b})^a$$

正是所要证的。

107

最后，假设 $a < 0$。则 $(-a)/b = (-a')/b'$。但 -a 是正的，所以前面的情况适用，我们有 $(x^{1/b'})^{-a'} = (x^{1/b})^{-a}$。取两边倒数可得结果。

□

因此对每个有理数 q，$x^q$ 都是良定义的。注意这个新定义与我们旧的 $x^{1/n}$ 定义是一致的（为什么？），也与旧的 $x^n$ 定义是一致的（为什么？）。

关于有理指数运算的一些基本事实：

引理 5.6.9 设 $x, y > 0$ 为正实数，$q, r$ 为有理数。

(a) $x^q$ 是正实数。

(b) $x^{q+r} = x^q x^r$ 且 $(x^q)^r = x^{qr}$。

(c) $x^{-q} = 1/x^q$。

(d) 若 $q > 0$，则 $x > y$ 当且仅当 $x^q > y^q$。

(e) 若 $x > 1$，则 $x^q > x^r$ 当且仅当 $q > r$。若 $x < 1$，则 $x^q > x^r$ 当且仅当 $q < r$。

(f) $(xy)^q = x^q y^q$。

证明 参见习题 5.6.2。

□

我们仍然需要进行实数指数运算；换句话说，我们仍然需要定义 $x^y$，其中 $x > 0$ 且 y 为实数——但我们将推迟到 6.7 节，一旦我们形式化极限的概念之后。

在本文的其余部分，我们将假设实数满足所有通常的代数定律、次序定律和指数定律。

习题 5.6.1 证明引理 5.6.6。（提示：复习命题 5.5.12 的证明。另外，反证法是一个有用的工具，特别是结合命题 5.4.7 和命题 5.4.12 中的次序三分律。引理的较前部分可以用来证明较后部分。对于(e)部分，先证明若 $x > 1$ 则 $x^{1/n} > 1$，若 $x < 1$ 则 $x^{1/n} < 1$。）

习题 5.6.2 证明引理 5.6.9。（提示：你主要应该依赖于引理 5.6.6 和代数运算。）

习题 5.6.3 若 x 是实数，n 是偶自然数（因此 $n = 2m$ 对某个自然数 m），证明 $x^n \geq 0$。

习题 5.6.4 若 x 是实数，证明 $|x| = (x^2)^{1/2}$。

习题 5.6.5 若 x, y 为正实数，q 为满足 $q \geq 1$ 的正有理数，证明 $\max(x^q, y^q) = \max(x, y)^q$ 和 $\min(x^q, y^q) = \min(x, y)^q$，其中 min、max 运算在习题 5.4.9 中定义。如果我们有 $q < 1$ 而不是 $q \geq 1$，会怎样？

Chapter 6
Limits of Sequences
6.1
Convergence and Limit Laws
In the previous chapter, we defined the real numbers as formal limits of rational (Cauchy) sequences, and we then defined various operations on the real numbers. However, unlike our work in constructing the integers (where we eventually
replaced formal differences with actual differences) and rationals (where we eventually replaced formal quotients with actual quotients), we did not completely finish
the job of constructing the real numbers, because we never got around to replacing
formal limits LIMn\to\inftyan with actual limits limn\to\inftyan. In fact, we haven’t defined
limits at all yet. This will now be rectified.
We begin by repeating much of the machinery of \varepsilon-close sequences, etc., again—
but this time, we do it for sequences of real numbers, not rational numbers. Thus
this discussion will supercede what we did in the previous chapter. First, we define
distance for real numbers:
Definition 6.1.1 (Distance between two real numbers). Given two real numbers x
and y, we define their distance d(x, y) to be d(x, y) := |x −y|.
Clearly this definition is consistent with Definition 4.3.2. Further, Proposition
4.3.3 works just as well for real numbers as it does for rationals, because the real
numbers obey all the rules of algebra that the rationals do.
Definition 6.1.2 (\varepsilon-close real numbers). Let \varepsilon > 0 be a real number. We say that
two real numbers x, y are \varepsilon-close iff we have d(y, x) \leq\varepsilon.
Again, it is clear that this definition of \varepsilon-close is consistent with Definition 4.3.4.
Now let (an)\infty
n=m be a sequence of real numbers; i.e., we assign a real number
an for every integer n \geqm. The starting index m is some integer; usually this will
be 1, but in some cases we will start from some index other than 1. (The choice of
label used to index this sequence is unimportant; we could use for instance (ak)\infty
k=m
and this would represent exactly the same sequence as (an)\infty
n=m.) We can define the
notion of a Cauchy sequence in the same manner as before.
© Hindustan Book Agency 2022
T. Tao, Analysis I, Texts and Readings in Mathematics,
https://doi.org/10.1007/978-981-19-7261-4_6
109
110
6
Limits of Sequences
Definition 6.1.3 (Cauchy sequences of reals). Let \varepsilon > 0 be a real number. A
sequence (an)\infty
n=N of real numbers starting at some integer index N is said to be
\varepsilon-steady iff a j and ak are \varepsilon-close for every j, k \geqN. A sequence (an)\infty
n=m starting at
some integer index m is said to be eventually \varepsilon-steady iff there exists an N \geqm such
that (an)\infty
n=N is \varepsilon-steady. We say that (an)\infty
n=m is a Cauchy sequence iff it is eventually
\varepsilon-steady for every \varepsilon > 0.
To put it another way, a sequence (an)\infty
n=m of real numbers is a Cauchy sequence
if, for every real \varepsilon > 0, there exists an N \geqm such that |an −an′| \leq\varepsilon for all n, n′ \geq
N. These definitions are consistent with the corresponding definitions for rational
numbers (Definitions 5.1.3, 5.1.6, 5.1.8), although verifying consistency for Cauchy
sequences takes a little bit of care.
Proposition 6.1.4 Let (an)\infty
n=m be a sequence of rational numbers starting at some
integer index m. Then (an)\infty
n=m is a Cauchy sequence in the sense of Definition 5.1.8
if and only if it is a Cauchy sequence in the sense of Definition 6.1.3.
Proof Suppose first that (an)\infty
n=m is a Cauchy sequence in the sense of Definition
6.1.3; then it is eventually \varepsilon-steady for every real \varepsilon > 0. In particular, it is eventually
\varepsilon-steady for every rational \varepsilon > 0, which makes it a Cauchy sequence in the sense of
Definition 5.1.8.
Now suppose that (an)\infty
n=m is a Cauchy sequence in the sense of Definition 5.1.8;
then it is eventually \varepsilon-steady for every rational \varepsilon > 0. If \varepsilon > 0 is a real number, then
there exists a rational \varepsilon′ > 0 which is smaller than \varepsilon, by Proposition 5.4.12. Since \varepsilon′
is rational, we know that (an)\infty
n=m is eventually \varepsilon′-steady; since \varepsilon′ < \varepsilon, this implies
that (an)\infty
n=m is eventually \varepsilon-steady. Since \varepsilon is an arbitrary positive real number, we
thus see that (an)\infty
n=m is a Cauchy sequence in the sense of Definition 6.1.3.
□
Because of this proposition, we will no longer care about the distinction between
Definition 5.1.8 and Definition 6.1.3 and view the concept of a Cauchy sequence as
a single unified concept.
Now we talk about what it means for a sequence of real numbers to converge to
some limit L.
Definition 6.1.5 (Convergence of sequences). Let \varepsilon > 0 be a real number, and let
L be a real number. A sequence (an)\infty
n=N of real numbers is said to be \varepsilon-close to L
iff an is \varepsilon-close to L for every n \geqN, i.e., we have |an −L| \leq\varepsilon for every n \geqN.
We say that a sequence (an)\infty
n=m is eventually \varepsilon-close to L iff there exists an N \geqm
such that (an)\infty
n=N is \varepsilon-close to L. We say that a sequence (an)\infty
n=m converges to L iff
it is eventually \varepsilon-close to L for every real \varepsilon > 0.
One can unwrap all the definitions here and write the concept of convergence
more directly; see Exercise 6.1.2.
Example 6.1.6 The sequence
0.9, 0.99, 0.999, 0.9999, . . .
6.1 Convergence and Limit Laws
111
is 0.1-close to 1 but is not 0.01-close to 1, because of the first element of the sequence.
However, it is eventually 0.01-close to 1. In fact, for every real \varepsilon > 0, this sequence
is eventually \varepsilon-close to 1, hence is convergent to 1.
Proposition 6.1.7 (Uniqueness of limits). Let (an)\infty
n=m be a real sequence starting
at some integer index m, and let L ̸= L′ be two distinct real numbers. Then it is not
possible for (an)\infty
n=m to converge to L while also converging to L′.
Proof Suppose for sake of contradiction that (an)\infty
n=m was converging to both L and
L′. Let \varepsilon = |L −L′|/3; note that \varepsilon is positive since L ̸= L′. Since (an)\infty
n=m converges
to L, weknowthat (an)\infty
n=m is eventually\varepsilon-closeto L; thus thereis an N \geqm suchthat
d(an, L) \leq\varepsilon for all n \geqN. Similarly, there is an M \geqm such that d(an, L′) \leq\varepsilon for
all n \geqM. In particular, if we set n := max(N, M), then we have d(an, L) \leq\varepsilon and
d(an, L′) \leq\varepsilon, hence by the triangle inequality d(L, L′) \leq2\varepsilon = 2|L −L′|/3. But
then we have |L −L′| \leq2|L −L′|/3, which contradicts the fact that |L −L′| > 0.
Thus it is not possible to converge to both L and L′.
□
Now that we know limits are unique, we can set up notation to specify them:
Definition 6.1.8 (Limits of sequences). If a sequence (an)\infty
n=m converges to some
real number L, we say that (an)\infty
n=m is convergent and that its limit is L; we write
L = lim
n\to\inftyan
to denote this fact. If a sequence (an)\infty
n=m is not converging to any real number L, we
say that the sequence (an)\infty
n=m is divergent and we leave limn\to\inftyan undefined.
Note that Proposition 6.1.7 ensures that a sequence can have at most one limit.
Thus, if the limit exists, it is a single real number, otherwise it is undefined.
Remark 6.1.9 The notation limn\to\inftyan does not give any indication about the starting index m of the sequence, but the starting index is irrelevant (Exercise 6.1.3). Thus
in the rest of this discussion we shall not be too careful as to where these sequences
start, as we shall be mostly focused on their limits.
We sometimes use the phrase “an \tox as n \to\infty” as an alternate way of writing
the statement “(an)\infty
n=m converges to x”. Bear in mind, though, that the individual
statements an \tox and n \to\inftydo not have any rigorous meaning; this phrase is
just a convention, though of course a very suggestive one.
Remark 6.1.10 The exact choice of letter used to denote the index (in this case n)
is irrelevant: the phrase limn\to\inftyan has exactly the same meaning as limk\to\inftyak,
for instance. Sometimes it will be convenient to change the label of the index to
avoid conflicts of notation; for instance, we might want to change n to k because n is
simultaneously being used for some other purpose, and we want to reduce confusion.
See Exercise 6.1.4.
112
6
Limits of Sequences
As an example of a limit, we present
Proposition 6.1.11 We have limn\to\infty1/n = 0.
Proof Wehavetoshowthatthesequence(an)\infty
n=1 convergesto0,wherean := 1/n.In
other words, for every \varepsilon > 0, we need to show that the sequence (an)\infty
n=1 is eventually
\varepsilon-close to 0. So, let \varepsilon > 0 be an arbitrary real number. We have to find an N such
that |an −0| \leq\varepsilon for every n \geqN. But if n \geqN, then
|an −0| = |1/n −0| = 1/n \leq1/N.
Thus, if we pick N > 1/\varepsilon (which we can do by the Archimedean principle), then
1/N < \varepsilon, and so (an)\infty
n=N is \varepsilon-close to 0. Thus (an)\infty
n=1 is eventually \varepsilon-close to 0.
Since \varepsilon was arbitrary, (an)\infty
n=1 converges to 0.
□
Proposition 6.1.12 (Convergent sequences are Cauchy). Suppose that (an)\infty
n=m is a
convergent sequence of real numbers. Then (an)\infty
n=m is also a Cauchy sequence.
Proof See Exercise 6.1.5.
□
Example 6.1.13 The sequence 1, −1, 1, −1, 1, −1, . . . is not a Cauchy sequence
(because it is not eventually 1-steady), and is hence not a convergent sequence, by
Proposition 6.1.12.
Remark 6.1.14 For a converse to Proposition 6.1.12, see Theorem 6.4.18.
Now we show that formal limits can be superceded by actual limits, just as formal
subtraction was superceded by actual subtraction when constructing the integers,
and formal division superceded by actual division when constructing the rational
numbers.
Proposition 6.1.15 (Formal limits are genuine limits). Suppose that (an)\infty
n=1 is a
Cauchy sequence of rational numbers. Then (an)\infty
n=1 converges to LIMn\to\inftyan, i.e.
LIM
n\to\inftyan = lim
n\to\inftyan.
Proof See Exercise 6.1.6.
□
Definition 6.1.16 (Bounded sequences). A sequence (an)\infty
n=m of real numbers is
bounded by a real number M iff we have |an| \leqM for all n \geqm. We say that
(an)\infty
n=m is bounded iff it is bounded by M for some real number M > 0.
This definition is consistent with Definition 5.1.12; see Exercise 6.1.7.
Recall from Lemma 5.1.15 that every Cauchy sequence of rational numbers is
bounded. An inspection of the proof of that Lemma shows that the same argument
works for real numbers; every Cauchy sequence of real numbers is bounded. In
particular, from Proposition 6.1.12 we have

Corollary 6.1.17 Every convergent sequence of real numbers is bounded.
Example 6.1.18 The sequence 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, . . . is not bounded, and hence is not
convergent.

— Exercises —
Exercise 6.1.1 Let (an)\infty
n=0 be a sequence of real numbers, such that an+1 > an for each natural
number n. Prove that whenever n and m are natural numbers such that m > n, then we have am > an.
(We refer to these sequences as strictly increasing sequences.)
Exercise 6.1.2 Let (an)\infty

(an)\infty

Exercise 6.1.6 Prove Proposition 6.1.15, using the following outline. Let (an)\infty
n=1 be a Cauchy
sequence of rationals, and write L := LIMn\to\inftyan. We have to show that (an)\infty
n=1 converges to L.
Let \varepsilon > 0. Assume for sake of contradiction that sequence an is not eventually \varepsilon-close to L. Use this,
and the fact that (an)\infty
n=1 is Cauchy, to show that there is an N \geqm such that either an > L + \varepsilon/2
for all n \geqN, or an < L −\varepsilon/2 for all n \geqN. Then use Exercise 5.4.8.
Exercise 6.1.7 Show that Definition 6.1.16 is consistent with Definition 5.1.12 (i.e., prove an
analogue of Proposition 6.1.4 for bounded sequences instead of Cauchy sequences).
Exercise 6.1.8 Prove Theorem 6.1.19. (Hint: you can use some parts of the theorem to prove others,
e.g., (b) can be used to prove (c); (a),(c) can be used to prove (d); and (b), (e) can be used to prove
(f). The proofs are similar to those of Lemma 5.3.6, Proposition 5.3.10, and Lemma 5.3.15. For (e),
you may need to first prove the auxiliary result that any sequence whose elements are non-zero, and
which converges to a non-zero limit, is bounded away from zero.)
Exercise 6.1.9 Explain why Theorem 6.1.19(f) fails when the limit of the denominator is 0. (To
repair that problem requires L’Hôpital’s rule, see Section 10.5.)
Exercise 6.1.10 Show that the concept of equivalent Cauchy sequence, as defined in Definition
5.2.6, does not change if \varepsilon is required to be positive real instead of positive rational. More precisely,
if (an)\infty
n=0 and (bn)\infty
n=0 are sequences of reals, show that (an)\infty
n=0 and (bn)\infty
n=0 are eventually \varepsilon-close
for every rational \varepsilon > 0 if and only if they are eventually \varepsilon-close for every real \varepsilon > 0. (Hint: modify
the proof of Proposition 6.1.4.)

# 第6章
# 序列的极限
## 6.1 收敛与极限定律

在上一章中，我们将实数定义为有理数（柯西）序列的形式极限，然后我们在实数上定义了各种运算。然而，与我们构造整数的工作（最终用实际差值替换了形式差值）和有理数的工作（最终用实际商替换了形式商）不同，我们没有完全完成实数的构造，因为我们从未涉及到用实际极限 $\lim_{n\to\infty}a_n$ 替换形式极限 $\operatorname{LIM}_{n\to\infty}a_n$。事实上，我们还没有定义过极限。现在这个问题将得到解决。

我们首先重复 $\varepsilon$-接近序列等的大部分框架——但这次，我们处理的是实数序列，而不是有理数序列。因此，这部分讨论将取代上一章的内容。首先，我们定义实数之间的距离：

**定义 6.1.1**（两个实数之间的距离）。给定两个实数 $x$ 和 $y$，我们定义它们的距离 $d(x, y)$ 为 $d(x, y) := |x - y|$。

显然这个定义与定义 4.3.2 一致。此外，命题 4.3.3 对实数的适用程度与对有理数一样好，因为实数服从有理数所遵循的所有代数法则。

**定义 6.1.2**（$\varepsilon$-接近的实数）。设 $\varepsilon > 0$ 为实数。我们称两个实数 $x, y$ 是 $\varepsilon$-接近的，当且仅当 $d(y, x) \leq \varepsilon$。

同样，$\varepsilon$-接近的这一定义与定义 4.3.4 是一致的。

现在设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 为一实数序列；即我们为每个整数 $n \geq m$ 指定一个实数 $a_n$。起始指标 $m$ 是某个整数；通常这是 1，但在某些情况下我们将从 1 以外的指标开始。（用于索引此序列的标签选择不重要；例如，我们可以使用 $(a_k)_{k=m}^{\infty}$，这与 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 表示完全相同的序列。）我们可以像之前一样定义柯西序列的概念。

© Hindustan Book Agency 2022
T. Tao, Analysis I, Texts and Readings in Mathematics,
https://doi.org/10.1007/978-981-19-7261-4_6
109
110
6
序列的极限

**定义 6.1.3**（实数的柯西序列）。设 $\varepsilon > 0$ 为实数。一个从某个整数指标 $N$ 开始的实数序列 $(a_n)_{n=N}^{\infty}$ 称为 $\varepsilon$-稳定的，当且仅当对每个 $j, k \geq N$，$a_j$ 和 $a_k$ 都是 $\varepsilon$-接近的。称一个从某个整数指标 $m$ 开始的序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是最终 $\varepsilon$-稳定的，当且仅当存在 $N \geq m$ 使得 $(a_n)_{n=N}^{\infty}$ 是 $\varepsilon$-稳定的。我们称 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是柯西序列，当且仅当它对每个 $\varepsilon > 0$ 都是最终 $\varepsilon$-稳定的。

换句话说，一列从某个整数指标 $m$ 开始的实数序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是柯西序列，当且仅当对每个实数 $\varepsilon > 0$，存在 $N \geq m$ 使得对所有 $n, n' \geq N$ 都有 $|a_n - a_{n'}| \leq \varepsilon$。这些定义与有理数相应的定义（定义 5.1.3、5.1.6、5.1.8）是一致的，尽管验证柯西序列的一致性需要稍加注意。

**命题 6.1.4** 设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是从某个整数指标 $m$ 开始的有理数序列。那么 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是定义 5.1.8 意义上的柯西序列，当且仅当它是定义 6.1.3 意义上的柯西序列。

**证明** 首先假设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是定义 6.1.3 意义上的柯西序列；那么它对每个实数 $\varepsilon > 0$ 都是最终 $\varepsilon$-稳定的。特别地，它对每个有理数 $\varepsilon > 0$ 都是最终 $\varepsilon$-稳定的，这使得它成为定义 5.1.8 意义上的柯西序列。

现在假设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是定义 5.1.8 意义上的柯西序列；那么它对每个有理数 $\varepsilon > 0$ 都是最终 $\varepsilon$-稳定的。如果 $\varepsilon > 0$ 是实数，由命题 5.4.12，存在小于 $\varepsilon$ 的有理数 $\varepsilon' > 0$。由于 $\varepsilon'$ 是有理数，我们知道 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是最终 $\varepsilon'$-稳定的；由于 $\varepsilon' < \varepsilon$，这意味着 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是最终 $\varepsilon$-稳定的。由于 $\varepsilon$ 是任意正实数，因此我们看到 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是定义 6.1.3 意义上的柯西序列。

□

由于这个命题，我们将不再关心定义 5.1.8 和定义 6.1.3 之间的区别，并将柯西序列的概念视为一个统一的单一概念。

现在我们讨论实数序列收敛到某个极限 $L$ 的含义。

**定义 6.1.5**（序列的收敛）。设 $\varepsilon > 0$ 为实数，$L$ 为实数。称实数序列 $(a_n)_{n=N}^{\infty}$ 是 $\varepsilon$-接近 $L$ 的，当且仅当对每个 $n \geq N$，$a_n$ 都与 $L$ 是 $\varepsilon$-接近的，即对每个 $n \geq N$ 有 $|a_n - L| \leq \varepsilon$。我们称序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是最终 $\varepsilon$-接近 $L$ 的，当且仅当存在 $N \geq m$ 使得 $(a_n)_{n=N}^{\infty}$ 是 $\varepsilon$-接近 $L$ 的。我们称序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $L$，当且仅当它对每个实数 $\varepsilon > 0$ 都是最终 $\varepsilon$-接近 $L$ 的。

可以将这里的所有定义展开，直接写出收敛的概念；见练习 6.1.2。

**例 6.1.6** 序列

$$0.9, 0.99, 0.999, 0.9999, \ldots$$

6.1 收敛与极限定律
111

是 $0.1$-接近 $1$ 的，但不是 $0.01$-接近 $1$ 的，因为序列的第一个元素。然而，它是最终 $0.01$-接近 $1$ 的。实际上，对每个实数 $\varepsilon > 0$，这个序列都是最终 $\varepsilon$-接近 $1$ 的，因此收敛到 $1$。

**命题 6.1.7**（极限的唯一性）。设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是从某个整数指标 $m$ 开始的实数序列，$L \neq L'$ 是两个不同的实数。那么 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 不可能同时收敛到 $L$ 和 $L'$。

**证明** 假设矛盾地设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 同时收敛到 $L$ 和 $L'$。设 $\varepsilon = |L - L'|/3$；注意由于 $L \neq L'$，$\varepsilon$ 是正的。由于 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $L$，我们知道 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是最终 $\varepsilon$-接近 $L$ 的；因此存在 $N \geq m$ 使得对所有 $n \geq N$ 有 $d(a_n, L) \leq \varepsilon$。类似地，存在 $M \geq m$ 使得对所有 $n \geq M$ 有 $d(a_n, L') \leq \varepsilon$。特别地，如果我们取 $n := \max(N, M)$，则有 $d(a_n, L) \leq \varepsilon$ 和 $d(a_n, L') \leq \varepsilon$，于是由三角不等式 $d(L, L') \leq 2\varepsilon = 2|L - L'|/3$。但这给我们 $|L - L'| \leq 2|L - L'|/3$，这与 $|L - L'| > 0$ 矛盾。因此不可能同时收敛到 $L$ 和 $L'$。

□

既然我们知道极限是唯一的，我们就可以建立符号来表示它们：

**定义 6.1.8**（序列的极限）。如果序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛到某个实数 $L$，我们称 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是收敛的，并称其极限为 $L$；我们写作

$$L = \lim_{n\to\infty}a_n$$

来表示这一事实。如果序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 不收敛到任何实数 $L$，我们称序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是发散的，并使 $\lim_{n\to\infty}a_n$ 保持未定义。

注意命题 6.1.7 确保了一个序列最多只能有一个极限。因此，如果极限存在，它是一个单一的实数；否则它是未定义的。

**注 6.1.9** 符号 $\lim_{n\to\infty}a_n$ 没有给出序列起始指标 $m$ 的任何指示，但起始指标是无关紧要的（见练习 6.1.3）。因此在接下来的讨论中，我们不会过于在意这些序列从哪里开始，因为我们主要关注的是它们的极限。

我们有时使用短语 "$a_n \to x$ 当 $n \to \infty$" 作为语句 "$(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $x$" 的另一种表述方式。但请注意，单独的陈述 "$a_n \to x$" 和 "$n \to \infty$" 没有严格的意义；这个短语只是一个约定，尽管当然是一个非常提示性的约定。

**注 6.1.10** 用来表示指标的字母选择是无关的：短语 $\lim_{n\to\infty}a_n$ 与 $\lim_{k\to\infty}a_k$ 的含义完全相同，例如。有时为了方便会更改指标的标签以避免符号冲突；例如，我们可能想把 $n$ 改成 $k$，因为 $n$ 同时被用于其他目的，我们想减少混淆。见练习 6.1.4。

112
6
序列的极限

作为一个极限的例子，我们给出

**命题 6.1.11** 我们有 $\lim_{n\to\infty}1/n = 0$。

**证明** 我们必须证明序列 $(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $0$，其中 $a_n := 1/n$。换句话说，对每个 $\varepsilon > 0$，我们需要证明序列 $(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 是最终 $\varepsilon$-接近 $0$ 的。因此，设 $\varepsilon > 0$ 为任意实数。我们必须找到 $N$ 使得对每个 $n \geq N$ 有 $|a_n - 0| \leq \varepsilon$。但如果 $n \geq N$，那么

$$|a_n - 0| = |1/n - 0| = 1/n \leq 1/N。$$

因此，如果我们选取 $N > 1/\varepsilon$（由阿基米德原理我们可以做到这一点），那么 $1/N < \varepsilon$，于是 $(a_n)_{n=N}^{\infty}$ 是 $\varepsilon$-接近 $0$ 的。因此 $(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 是最终 $\varepsilon$-接近 $0$ 的。由于 $\varepsilon$ 是任意的，$(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $0$。

□

**命题 6.1.12**（收敛序列是柯西序列）。设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是实数的收敛序列。那么 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 也是柯西序列。

**证明** 见练习 6.1.5。

□

**例 6.1.13** 序列 $1, -1, 1, -1, 1, -1, \ldots$ 不是柯西序列（因为它不是最终 $1$-稳定的），因此由命题 6.1.12，它也不是收敛序列。

**注 6.1.14** 关于命题 6.1.12 的逆命题，见定理 6.4.18。

现在我们证明形式极限可以被实际极限所取代，正如在构造整数时形式减法被实际减法所取代，以及在构造有理数时形式除法被实际除法所取代一样。

**命题 6.1.15**（形式极限是真实极限）。设 $(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 是有理数的柯西序列。那么 $(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $\operatorname{LIM}_{n\to\infty}a_n$，即

$$\operatorname{LIM}_{n\to\infty}a_n = \lim_{n\to\infty}a_n。$$

**证明** 见练习 6.1.6。

□

**定义 6.1.16**（有界序列）。设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是实数序列。如果对所有 $n \geq m$ 有 $|a_n| \leq M$，我们称该序列被实数 $M$ 所界。我们称 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是有界的，当且仅当它被某个实数 $M > 0$ 所界。

这个定义与定义 5.1.12 是一致的；见练习 6.1.7。

回忆引理 5.1.15，每个有理数的柯西序列是有界的。检查该引理的证明可知，同样的论证对实数也成立；每个实数的柯西序列是有界的。特别地，由命题 6.1.12 我们有

6.1 收敛与极限定律
113

**推论 6.1.17** 每个实数的收敛序列是有界的。

**例 6.1.18** 序列 $1, 2, 3, 4, 5, \ldots$ 不是有界的，因此不是收敛的。

现在我们可以证明通常的极限定律。

**定理 6.1.19**（极限定律）。设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 和 $(b_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是实数的收敛序列，设 $x, y$ 是实数 $x := \lim_{n\to\infty}a_n$ 和 $y := \lim_{n\to\infty}b_n$。

(a) 序列 $(a_n + b_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $x + y$；换句话说，

$$\lim_{n\to\infty}(a_n + b_n) = \lim_{n\to\infty}a_n + \lim_{n\to\infty}b_n。$$

(b) 序列 $(a_n b_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $xy$；换句话说，

$$\lim_{n\to\infty}(a_n b_n) = \left(\lim_{n\to\infty}a_n\right) \left(\lim_{n\to\infty}b_n\right)。$$

(c) 对任意实数 $c$，序列 $(c a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $cx$；换句话说，

$$\lim_{n\to\infty}(c a_n) = c \lim_{n\to\infty}a_n。$$

(d) 序列 $(a_n - b_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $x - y$；换句话说，

$$\lim_{n\to\infty}(a_n - b_n) = \lim_{n\to\infty}a_n - \lim_{n\to\infty}b_n。$$

(e) 设 $y \neq 0$，且对所有 $n \geq m$ 有 $b_n \neq 0$。那么序列 $(b_n^{-1})_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $y^{-1}$；换句话说，

$$\lim_{n\to\infty}b_n^{-1} = \left(\lim_{n\to\infty}b_n\right)^{-1}。$$

(f) 设 $y \neq 0$，且对所有 $n \geq m$ 有 $b_n \neq 0$。那么序列 $(a_n/b_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $x/y$；换句话说，

$$\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{a_n}{b_n} = \frac{\lim_{n\to\infty}a_n}{\lim_{n\to\infty}b_n}。$$

(g) 序列 $(\max(a_n, b_n))_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $\max(x, y)$；换句话说，

$$\lim_{n\to\infty}\max(a_n, b_n) = \max\left(\lim_{n\to\infty}a_n, \lim_{n\to\infty}b_n\right)。$$

(h) 序列 $(\min(a_n, b_n))_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $\min(x, y)$；换句话说，

$$\lim_{n\to\infty}\min(a_n, b_n) = \min\left(\lim_{n\to\infty}a_n, \lim_{n\to\infty}b_n\right)。$$

114
6
序列的极限

**证明** 见练习 6.1.8。

□

—— 练习 ——

**练习 6.1.1** 设 $(a_n)_{n=0}^{\infty}$ 是实数序列，使得对每个自然数 $n$ 有 $a_{n+1} > a_n$。证明每当 $m > n$ 是自然数时，我们有 $a_m > a_n$。（我们称这些序列为严格递增序列。）

**练习 6.1.2** 设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是实数序列，$L$ 是实

6.2 The Extended Real Number System
115
6.2
The Extended Real Number System
There are some sequences which do not converge to any real number but instead
seem to be wanting to converge to +\inftyor −\infty. For instance, it seems intuitive that
the sequence
1, 2, 3, 4, 5, . . .
should be converging to +\infty, while
−1, −2, −3, −4, −5, . . .
should be converging to −\infty. Meanwhile, the sequence
1, −1, 1, −1, 1, −1, . . .
does not seem to be converging to anything (although we shall see later that it does
have +1 and −1 as “limit points”—see below). Similarly the sequence
1, −2, 3, −4, 5, −6, . . .
does not converge to any real number, and also does not appear to be converging to
+\inftyor converging to −\infty. To make this precise we need to talk about something
called the extended real number system.
Definition 6.2.1 (Extended real number system). The extended real number system
R∗is the real line R with two additional elements attached, called +\inftyand −\infty.
These elements are distinct from each other and also distinct from every real number.
An extended real number x is called finite iff it is a real number, and infinite iff it is
equal to +\inftyor −\infty. (This definition is not directly related to the notion of finite
and infinite sets in Section 3.6, though it is of course similar in spirit.)
These new symbols, +\inftyand −\infty, at present do not have much meaning, since
we have no operations to manipulate them (other than equality = and inequality
̸=). As with many of the other mathematical concepts considered here, the precise
construction of +\inftyand −\inftyis not important, but (by Exercise 3.2.2) one could
for instance set +\infty:= {R} and −\infty:= {R \cup{\infty}} if desired. Now we place a few
operations on the extended real number system.
Definition 6.2.2 (Negation of extended reals). The operation of negation x 
\to−x
on R, we now extend to R∗by defining −(+\infty) := −\inftyand −(−\infty) := +\infty.
Thus every extended real number x has a negation, and −(−x) is always equal to
x.
Definition 6.2.3 (Ordering of extended reals). Let x and y be extended real numbers.
We say that x \leqy, i.e., x is less than or equal to y, iff one of the following three
statements is true:
116
6
Limits of Sequences
(a) x and y are real numbers, and x \leqy as real numbers.
(b) y = +\infty.
(c) x = −\infty.
We say that x < y if we have x \leqy and x ̸= y. We sometimes write x < y as
y > x, and x \leqy as y \geqx.
Example 6.2.4 3 \leq5, 3 < +\infty, and −\infty< +\infty, but 3 ≰−\infty.
Some basic properties of order and negation on the extended real number system:
Proposition 6.2.5 Let x, y, z be extended real numbers. Then the following statements are true:
(a) (Reflexivity) We have x \leqx.
(b) (Trichotomy) Exactly one of the statements x < y, x = y, or x > y is true.
(c) (Transitivity) If x \leqy and y \leqz, then x \leqz.
(d) (Negation reverses order) If x \leqy, then −y \leq−x.
Proof See Exercise 6.2.1.
□
One could also introduce other operations on the extended real number system,
such as addition and multiplication. However, this is somewhat dangerous as these
operations will almost certainly fail to obey the familiar rules of algebra. For instance,
to define addition it seems reasonable (given one’s intuitive notion of infinity) to set
+\infty+ 5 = +\inftyand +\infty+ 3 = +\infty, but then this implies that +\infty+ 5 = +\infty+
3, while 5 ̸= 3. So things like the cancelation law begin to break down once we try
to operate involving infinity. To avoid these issues we shall simply not define any
arithmetic operations on the extended real number system other than negation and
order.
Remember that we defined the notion of supremum or least upper bound of a
set E of reals; this gave an extended real number sup(E), which was either finite or
infinite. We now extend this notion slightly.
Definition 6.2.6 (Supremum of sets of extended reals). Let E be a subset of R∗.
Then we define the supremum sup(E) or least upper bound of E by the following
rule.
(a) If E is contained in R (i.e., +\inftyand −\inftyare not elements of E), then we let
sup(E) be as defined in Definition 5.5.10.
(b) If E contains +\infty, then we set sup(E) := +\infty.
(c) If E does not contain +\inftybut does contain −\infty, then we set sup(E) :=
sup(E\{−\infty}) (which is a subset of R and thus falls under case (a)).
We also define the infimum inf(E) of E (also known as the greatest lower bound
of E) by the formula
inf(E) := −sup(−E)
where −E is the set −E := {−x : x \inE}.
6.2 The Extended Real Number System
117
Example 6.2.7 Let E be the negative integers, together with −\infty:
E = {−1, −2, −3, −4, . . .} \cup{−\infty}.
Then sup(E) = sup(E\{−\infty}) = −1, while
inf(E) = −sup(−E) = −(+\infty) = −\infty.
Example 6.2.8 The set {0.9, 0.99, 0.999, 0.9999, . . .} has infimum 0.9 and supremum 1. Note that in this case the supremum does not actually belong to the set, but
it is in some sense “touching it” from the right.
Example 6.2.9 The set {1, 2, 3, 4, 5 . . .} has infimum 1 and supremum +\infty.
Example 6.2.10 Let E be the empty set. Then sup(E) = −\inftyand inf(E) = +\infty
(why?). This is the only case in which the supremum can be less than the infimum
(why?).
One can intuitively think of the supremum of E as follows. Imagine the real line
with +\inftysomehow on the far right, and −\inftyon the far left. Imagine a piston at +\infty
moving leftward until it is stopped by the presence of a set E; the location where
it stops is the supremum of E. Similarly if one imagines a piston at −\inftymoving
rightward until it is stopped by the presence of E, the location where it stops is the
infimum of E. In the case when E is the empty set, the pistons pass through each
other, the supremum landing at −\inftyand the infimum landing at +\infty.
The following theorem justifies the terminology “least upper bound” and “greatest
lower bound”:
Theorem 6.2.11 Let E be a subset of R∗. Then the following statements are true.
(a) For every x \inE we have x \leqsup(E) and x \geqinf(E).
(b) Suppose that M \inR∗is an upper bound for E, i.e., x \leqM for all x \inE. Then
we have sup(E) \leqM.
(c) Suppose that M \inR∗is a lower bound for E, i.e., x \geqM for all x \inE. Then
we have inf(E) \geqM.
Proof See Exercise 6.2.2.
□
— Exercises —
Exercise 6.2.1 Prove Proposition 6.2.5. (Hint: you may need Proposition 5.4.7.)
Exercise 6.2.2 Prove Theorem 6.2.11. (Hint: you may need to break into cases depending on
whether +\inftyor −\inftybelongs to E. You can of course use Definition 5.5.10, provided that E
consists only of real numbers.)
sections.content_zh:
6.2 扩展实数系

有些序列不收敛于任何实数，而是似乎要收敛到 $+\infty$ 或 $-\infty$。例如，序列

$$1, 2, 3, 4, 5, \ldots$$

似乎直观地应该收敛到 $+\infty$，而序列

$$-1, -2, -3, -4, -5, \ldots$$

似乎应该收敛到 $-\infty$。同时，序列

$$1, -1, 1, -1, 1, -1, \ldots$$

似乎不收敛于任何数（尽管我们后面会看到它确实有 $+1$ 和 $-1$ 作为"极限点"——见下文）。类似地，序列

$$1, -2, 3, -4, 5, -6, \ldots$$

不收敛于任何实数，也似乎不收敛到 $+\infty$ 或收敛到 $-\infty$。为了精确地描述这些现象，我们需要引入扩展实数系的概念。

**定义 6.2.1（扩展实数系）** 扩展实数系 $\mathbb{R}^*$ 是实数线 $\mathbb{R}$ 附加两个额外的元素构成，这两个元素称为 $+\infty$ 和 $-\infty$。这两个元素彼此不同，且与每个实数都不同。一个扩展实数 $x$ 称为有限的，当且仅当它是一个实数；称为无穷的，当且仅当它等于 $+\infty$ 或 $-\infty$。（这个定义与 3.6 节中有限集合和无限集合的概念没有直接关系，尽管在精神上当然是相似的。）

这些新的符号 $+\infty$ 和 $-\infty$，目前还没有太多含义，因为我们没有操作它们的运算（除了相等 $=$ 和不等 $\neq$）。与这里讨论的许多其他数学概念一样，$+\infty$ 和 $-\infty$ 的具体构造并不重要，但（根据练习 3.2.2）如果需要的话，例如可以设 $+\infty := \{\mathbb{R}\}$ 且 $-\infty := \{\mathbb{R} \cup \{\infty\}\}$。现在我们在扩展实数系上定义一些运算。

**定义 6.2.2（扩展实数的取负运算）** 实数集 $\mathbb{R}$ 上的取负运算 $x \mapsto -x$，现在扩展到 $\mathbb{R}^*$，定义为
$$-(+\infty) := -\infty \quad \text{和} \quad -(-\infty) := +\infty。$$
因此每个扩展实数 $x$ 都有其取负，且 $-(-x)$ 总是等于 $x$。

**定义 6.2.3（扩展实数的序关系）** 设 $x$ 和 $y$ 是扩展实数。我们说 $x \leq y$（即 $x$ 小于或等于 $y$），当且仅当以下三个陈述中有一个成立：

(a) $x$ 和 $y$ 都是实数，且作为实数有 $x \leq y$；

(b) $y = +\infty$；

(c) $x = -\infty$。

我们说 $x < y$ 如果有 $x \leq y$ 且 $x \neq y$。我们有时将 $x < y$ 写作 $y > x$，将 $x \leq y$ 写作 $y \geq x$。

**例 6.2.4** $3 \leq 5$，$3 < +\infty$，且 $-\infty < +\infty$，但 $3 \not\leq -\infty$。

扩展实数系上序关系和取负运算的一些基本性质：

**命题 6.2.5** 设 $x, y, z$ 是扩展实数。则以下陈述均为真：

(a)（自反性）$x \leq x$。

(b)（三分性）$x < y$、$x = y$ 和 $x > y$ 中恰好有一个为真。

(c)（传递性）如果 $x \leq y$ 且 $y \leq z$，则 $x \leq z$。

(d)（取负反转序关系）如果 $x \leq y$，则 $-y \leq -x$。

**证明** 见练习 6.2.1。

---

我们也可以在扩展实数系上引入其他运算，如加法和乘法。然而，这是相当危险的，因为这些运算很可能不服从熟悉的代数规则。例如，为了定义加法，考虑到对无穷的直观理解，设 $+\infty + 5 = +\infty$ 和 $+\infty + 3 = +\infty$ 似乎是合理的，但这样就推出了 $+\infty + 5 = +\infty + 3$，而 $5 \neq 3$。因此，一旦我们尝试涉及无穷的运算，像消去律这样的规则就开始失效了。为了避免这些问题，除了取负运算和序关系外，我们不在扩展实数系上定义任何算术运算。

回顾一下，我们定义了实数集 $E$ 的上确界或最小上界的概念；这给出了一个扩展实数 $\sup(E)$，它或者是有限的，或者是无限的。现在我们略微扩展这个概念。

**定义 6.2.6（扩展实数集的上确界）** 设 $E$ 是 $\mathbb{R}^*$ 的一个子集。则我们按下述规则定义 $E$ 的上确界 $\sup(E)$ 或最小上界：

(a) 如果 $E$ 包含在 $\mathbb{R}$ 中（即 $+\infty$ 和 $-\infty$ 都不是 $E$ 的元素），则设 $\sup(E)$ 如定义 5.5.10 中所定义。

(b) 如果 $E$ 包含 $+\infty$，则设 $\sup(E) := +\infty$。

(c) 如果 $E$ 不包含 $+\infty$ 但包含 $-\infty$，则设 $\sup(E) := \sup(E \setminus \{-\infty\})$（这是 $\mathbb{R}$ 的一个子集，因此属于情形 (a)）。

我们还按下式定义 $E$ 的下确界 $\inf(E)$（也称为最大下界）：
$$\inf(E) := -\sup(-E)$$
其中 $-E$ 是集合 $-E := \{-x : x \in E\}$。

**例 6.2.7** 设 $E$ 是负整数与 $-\infty$ 的并：
$$E = \{-1, -2, -3, -4, \ldots\} \cup \{-\infty\}。$$
则 $\sup(E) = \sup(E \setminus \{-\infty\}) = -1$，而
$$\inf(E) = -\sup(-E) = -(+\infty) = -\infty。$$

**例 6.2.8** 集合 $\{0.9, 0.99, 0.999, 0.9999, \ldots\}$ 的下确界是 $0.9$，上确界是 $1$。注意在这个例子中上确界实际上不属于该集合，但从某种意义上说它从右侧"触及"了该集合。

**例 6.2.9** 集合 $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, \ldots\}$ 的下确界是 $1$，上确界是 $+\infty$。

**例 6.2.10** 设 $E$ 是空集。则 $\sup(E) = -\infty$ 且 $\inf(E) = +\infty$（为什么？）。这是唯一上确界可能小于下确界的情形（为什么？）。

我们可以用以下方式直观地理解 $E$ 的上确界。设想实数线，其右端某处是 $+\infty$，左端某处是 $-\infty$。设想一个活塞从 $+\infty$ 处开始向左移动，直到被集合 $E$ 的存在所阻挡；它停止的位置就是 $E$ 的上确界。类似地，如果设想一个活塞从 $-\infty$ 处开始向右移动，直到被 $E$ 所阻挡，它停止的位置就是 $E$ 的下确界。在 $E$ 是空集的情形，两个活塞彼此穿过，上确界落在 $-\infty$ 而下确界落在 $+\infty$。

下面的定理为"最小上界"和"最大下界"这些术语提供了合理性依据：

**定理 6.2.11** 设 $E$ 是 $\mathbb{R}^*$ 的一个子集。则以下陈述为真：

(a) 对每个 $x \in E$，我们有 $x \leq \sup(E)$ 且 $x \geq \inf(E)$。

(b) 设 $M \in \mathbb{R}^*$ 是 $E$ 的一个上界，即对所有 $x \in E$ 有 $x \leq M$。则我们有 $\sup(E) \leq M$。

(c) 设 $M \in \mathbb{R}^*$ 是 $E$ 的一个下界，即对所有 $x \in E$ 有 $x \geq M$。则我们有 $\inf(E) \geq M$。

**证明** 见练习 6.2.2。

---

**练习**

**练习 6.2.1** 证明命题 6.2.5。（提示：你可能需要命题 5.4.7。）

**练习 6.2.2** 证明定理 6.2.11。（提示：你可能需要根据 $+\infty$ 或 $-\infty$ 是否属于 $E$ 来分类讨论。当然，你可以使用定义 5.5.10，前提是 $E$ 仅由实数组成。）

sections.title_zh: 数列的上确界与下确界

118
6
Limits of Sequences
6.3
Suprema and Infima of Sequences
Having defined the notion of a supremum and infimum of sets of reals, we can now
also talk about the supremum and infimum of a sequence.
Definition 6.3.1 (Suprema and infima of sequences). Let (an)\infty
n=m be a sequence of
real numbers. Then we define sup(an)\infty
n=m to be the supremum of the set {an : n \geqm},
and inf(an)\infty
n=m to the infimum of the same set {an : n \geqm}.
Remark 6.3.2 The quantities sup(an)\infty
n=m and inf(an)\infty
n=m are sometimes written as
supn\geqm an and infn\geqm an respectively.
Example 6.3.3 Let an := (−1)n; thus (an)\infty
n=1 is the sequence −1, 1, −1, 1, . . ..
Then the set {an : n \geq1} is just the two-element set {−1, 1}, and hence sup(an)\infty
n=1
is equal to 1. Similarly inf(an)\infty
n=1 is equal to −1.
Example 6.3.4 Let an := 1/n; thus (an)\infty
n=1 is the sequence 1, 1/2, 1/3, . . .. Then
the set {an : n \geq1} is the countable set {1, 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, . . .}. Thus sup(an)\infty
n=1 = 1
and inf(an)\infty
n=1 = 0 (Exercise 6.3.1). Notice here that the infimum of the sequence is
not actually a member of the sequence, though it becomes very close to the sequence
eventually. (So it is a little inaccurate to think of the supremum and infimum as the
“largest element of the sequence” and “smallest element of the sequence”, respectively.)
Example 6.3.5 Let an := n; thus (an)\infty
n=1 is the sequence 1, 2, 3, 4, . . .. Then the
set {an : n \geq1} is just the positive integers {1, 2, 3, 4, . . .}. Then sup(an)\infty
n=1 = +\infty
and inf(an)\infty
n=1 = 1.
As the last example shows, it is possible for the supremum or infimum of a
sequence to be +\inftyor −\infty. However, if a sequence (an)\infty
n=m is bounded, say bounded
by M, then all the elements an of the sequence lie between −M and M, so that the
set {an : n \geqm} has M as an upper bound and −M as a lower bound. Since this
set is clearly non-empty, we can thus conclude that the supremum and infimum of a
bounded sequence are real numbers (i.e., not +\inftyand −\infty).
Proposition 6.3.6 (Least upper bound property). Let (an)\infty
n=m be a sequence of real
numbers, and let x be the extended real number x := sup(an)\infty
n=m. Then we have
an \leqx for all n \geqm. Also, whenever M \inR∗is an upper bound for an (i.e., an \leqM
for all n \geqm), we have x \leqM. Finally, for every extended real number y for which
y < x, there exists at least one n \geqm for which y < an \leqx.
Proof See Exercise 6.3.2.
□
Remark 6.3.7 There is a corresponding Proposition for infima, but with all the references to order reversed, e.g., all upper bounds should now be lower bounds, etc.
The proof is exactly the same.
6.3 Suprema and Infima of Sequences
119
Now we give an application of these concepts of supremum and infimum. In the
previous section we saw that all convergent sequences are bounded. It is natural to ask
whether the converse is true: are all bounded sequences convergent? The answer is
no; for instance, the sequence 1, −1, 1, −1, . . . is bounded, but not Cauchy and hence
not convergent. However, if we make the sequence both bounded and monotone (i.e.,
increasing or decreasing), then it is true that it must converge:
Proposition 6.3.8 (Monotone bounded sequences converge). Let (an)\infty
n=m be a
sequence of real numbers which has some finite upper bound M \inR, and which
is also increasing (i.e., an+1 \geqan for all n \geqm). Then (an)\infty
n=m is convergent, and
in fact
lim
n\to\inftyan = sup(an)\infty
n=m \leqM.
Proof See Exercise 6.3.3.
□
One can similarly prove that if a sequence (an)\infty
n=m is bounded below and decreasing (i.e., an+1 \leqan), then it is convergent, and that the limit is equal to the infimum.
A sequence is said to be monotone if it is either increasing or decreasing. From
Proposition 6.3.8 and Corollary 6.1.17 we see that a monotone sequence converges
if and only if it is bounded.
Example 6.3.9 The sequence 3, 3.1, 3.14, 3.141, 3.1415, . . . is increasing, and is
bounded above by 4. Hence by Proposition 6.3.8 it must have a limit, which is a real
number less than or equal to 4.
Proposition 6.3.8 asserts that the limit of a monotone sequence exists, but does
not directly say what that limit is. Nevertheless, with a little extra work one can often
find the limit once one is given that the limit does exist. For instance:
Proposition 6.3.10 Let 0 < x < 1. Then we have limn\to\inftyxn = 0.
Proof Since 0 < x < 1, one can show that the sequence (xn)\infty
n=1 is decreasing
(why?). On the other hand, the sequence (xn)\infty
n=1 has a lower bound of 0. Thus by
Proposition 6.3.8 (for infima instead of suprema) the sequence (xn)\infty
n=1 converges to
some limit L. Since xn+1 = x \times xn, we thus see from the limit laws (Theorem 6.1.19)
that (xn+1)\infty
n=1 converges to xL. But the sequence (xn+1)\infty
n=1 is just the sequence
(xn)\infty
n=2 shifted by one, and so they must have the same limits (why?). So xL = L.
Since x ̸= 1, we can solve for L to obtain L = 0. Thus (xn)\infty
n=1 converges to 0.
□
Note that this proof does not work when x > 1 (Exercise 6.3.4).
— Exercises —
Exercise 6.3.1 Verify the claim in Example 6.3.4.
Exercise 6.3.2 Prove Proposition 6.3.6. (Hint: use Theorem 6.2.11.)
Exercise 6.3.3 Prove Proposition 6.3.8. (Hint: use Proposition 6.3.6, together with the assumption
that an is increasing, to show that an converges to sup(an)\infty
n=m.)
sections.content_zh:
# 6.3 序列的上确界与下确界

在定义了实数集的上确界与下确界的概念之后，我们现在也可以讨论序列的上确界与下确界。

**定义 6.3.1（序列的上确界与下确界）**。设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 为一列实数。则我们定义 $\sup(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 为集合 $\{a_n : n \geq m\}$ 的上确界，$\inf(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 为同一集合 $\{a_n : n \geq m\}$ 的下确界。

**注 6.3.2** 量 $\sup(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 和 $\inf(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 有时也分别写作 $\sup_{n \geq m} a_n$ 和 $\inf_{n \geq m} a_n$。

**例 6.3.3** 设 $a_n := (-1)^n$；则 $(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 是序列 $-1, 1, -1, 1, \ldots$。集合 $\{a_n : n \geq 1\}$ 只是一个二元集合 $\{-1, 1\}$，因此 $\sup(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 等于 $1$。类似地，$\inf(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 等于 $-1$。

**例 6.3.4** 设 $a_n := 1/n$；则 $(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 是序列 $1, 1/2, 1/3, \ldots$。集合 $\{a_n : n \geq 1\}$ 是可数集合 $\{1, 1/2, 1/3, 1/4, \ldots\}$。因此 $\sup(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty} = 1$ 且 $\inf(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty} = 0$（练习 6.3.1）。注意这里序列的下确界实际上并不是该序列的成员，尽管最终它与序列非常接近。（因此，把上确界和下确界分别理解为"序列的最大元素"和"序列的最小元素"是不太准确的。）

**例 6.3.5** 设 $a_n := n$；则 $(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 是序列 $1, 2, 3, 4, \ldots$。集合 $\{a_n : n \geq 1\}$ 就是正整数集合 $\{1, 2, 3, 4, \ldots\}$。则 $\sup(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty} = +\infty$ 且 $\inf(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty} = 1$。

如最后一个例子所示，序列的上确界或下确界有可能为 $+\infty$ 或 $-\infty$。然而，如果序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是有界的，比如被 $M$ 所界住，则序列的所有元素 $a_n$ 都落在 $-M$ 和 $M$ 之间，因此集合 $\{a_n : n \geq m\}$ 以 $M$ 为上界、$-M$ 为下界。由于该集合显然非空，我们可以得出结论：有界序列的上确界与下确界是实数（即不是 $+\infty$ 和 $-\infty$）。

**命题 6.3.6（上确界性质）**。设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 为一列实数，并设 $x$ 为扩展实数 $x := \sup(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$。则我们有 $a_n \leq x$ 对所有 $n \geq m$ 成立。同时，每当 $M \in \mathbb{R}^*$ 是 $a_n$ 的上界（即对所有 $n \geq m$ 有 $a_n \leq M$）时，我们有 $x \leq M$。最后，对于每个满足 $y < x$ 的扩展实数 $y$，存在至少一个 $n \geq m$ 使得 $y < a_n \leq x$。

**证明** 见练习 6.3.2。

□

**注 6.3.7** 存在一个关于下确界的对应命题，但所有关于顺序的描述都反转了，例如所有上界现在都变成下界，等等。证明完全相同。

---

现在我们给出这些上确界与下确界概念的一个应用。在上一节中我们看到所有收敛的序列都是有界的。很自然地会问其逆命题是否成立：是否所有有界序列都是收敛的？答案是否定的；例如，序列 $1, -1, 1, -1, \ldots$ 是有界的，但不是柯西序列，因此也不收敛。然而，如果我们在有界的基础上再加上单调性（即递增或递减），那么序列确实必定收敛：

**命题 6.3.8（单调有界序列收敛）**。设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 为一列实数，它有一个有限的上界 $M \in \mathbb{R}$，并且是递增的（即对所有 $n \geq m$ 有 $a_{n+1} \geq a_n$）。则 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛，且实际上

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n = \sup(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty} \leq M.$$

**证明** 见练习 6.3.3。

□

类似地，可以证明如果序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 有下界且递减（即对所有 $n \geq m$ 有 $a_{n+1} \leq a_n$），则它收敛，且极限等于下确界。

如果一个序列是递增或递减的，我们称其为**单调**的。由命题 6.3.8 和推论 6.1.17 可知，一个单调序列收敛当且仅当它是有界的。

**例 6.3.9** 序列 $3, 3.1, 3.14, 3.141, 3.1415, \ldots$ 是递增的，且被 $4$ 所上界。因此由命题 6.3.8，它必定有一个极限，该极限是一个不超过 $4$ 的实数。

命题 6.3.8 断言单调序列的极限存在，但并不直接给出该极限的值。然而，只要知道极限存在，通过一些额外的工作往往可以求出极限。例如：

**命题 6.3.10** 设 $0 < x < 1$。则我们有 $\lim_{n \to \infty} x^n = 0$。

**证明** 由于 $0 < x < 1$，可以证明序列 $(x^n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 是递减的（为什么？）。另一方面，序列 $(x^n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 有下界 $0$。因此由命题 6.3.8（下确界的情形），序列 $(x^n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 收敛到某个极限 $L$。由于 $x^{n+1} = x \times x^n$，从极限定律（定理 6.1.19）可知序列 $(x^{n+1})_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $xL$。但序列 $(x^{n+1})_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 实际上只是序列 $(x^n)_{n=2}^{\infty}$ 的平移，因此它们必定有相同的极限（为什么？）。于是 $xL = L$。由于 $x \neq 1$，我们可以解出 $L$ 得到 $L = 0$。因此 $(x^n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 收敛到 $0$。

□

注意这个证明在 $x > 1$ 时不适用（练习 6.3.4）。

---

**— 练习 —**

**练习 6.3.1** 验证例 6.3.4 中的结论。

**练习 6.3.2** 证明命题 6.3.6。（提示：利用定理 6.2.11。）

**练习 6.3.3** 证明命题 6.3.8。（提示：利用命题 6.3.6，结合 $a_n$ 递增的假设，证明 $a_n$ 收敛到 $\sup(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$。）

6
Limits of Sequences
Exercise 6.3.4 Explain why Proposition 6.3.10 fails when x > 1. In fact, show that the sequence
(xn)\infty
n=1 diverges when x > 1. (Hint: prove by contradiction and use the identity (1/x)nxn = 1 and
the limit laws in Theorem 6.1.19.) Compare this with the argument in Example 1.2.3; can you now
explain the flaws in the reasoning in that example?
6.4
Limsup, Liminf, and Limit Points
Consider the sequence
1.1, −1.01, 1.001, −1.0001, 1.00001, . . . .
If one plots this sequence, then one sees (informally, of course) that this sequence
does not converge; half the time the sequence is getting close to 1, and half the time
the sequence is getting close to −1, but it is not converging to either of them; for
instance, it never gets eventually 1/2-close to 1, and never gets eventually 1/2-close
to −1. However, even though −1 and +1 are not quite limits of this sequence, it does
seem that in some vague way they “want” to be limits. To make this notion precise
we introduce the notion of a limit point.
Definition 6.4.1 (Limit points). Let (an)\infty
n=m be a sequence of real numbers, let x be
a real number, and let \varepsilon > 0 be a real number. We say that x is \varepsilon-adherent to (an)\infty
n=m
iff there exists an n \geqm such that an is \varepsilon-close to x. We say that x is continually
\varepsilon-adherent to (an)\infty
n=m iff it is \varepsilon-adherent to (an)\infty
n=N for every N \geqm. We say that x
is a limit point or adherent point of (an)\infty
n=m iff it is continually \varepsilon-adherent to (an)\infty
n=m
for every \varepsilon > 0.
Remark 6.4.2 The verb “to adhere” means much the same as “to stick to”; hence
the term “adhesive”.
Unwrapping all the definitions, we see that x is a limit point of (an)\infty
n=m if, for
every \varepsilon > 0 and every N \geqm, there exists an n \geqN such that |an −x| \leq\varepsilon. (Why
is this the same definition?) Note the difference between a sequence being \varepsilon-close to
L (which means that all the elements of the sequence stay within a distance \varepsilon of L)
and L being \varepsilon-adherent to the sequence (which only needs a single element of the
sequence to stay within a distance \varepsilon of L). Also, for L to be continually \varepsilon-adherent to
(an)\infty
n=m, it has to be \varepsilon-adherent to (an)\infty
n=N for all N \geqm, whereas for (an)\infty
n=m to be
eventually \varepsilon-close to L, we only need (an)\infty
n=N to be \varepsilon-close to L for some N \geqm.
Thus there are some subtle differences in quantifiers between limits and limit points.
Note that limit points are only defined for finite real numbers. It is also possible to
rigorously define the concept of +\inftyor −\inftybeing a limit point; see Exercise 6.4.8.
Example 6.4.3 Let (an)\infty
n=1 denote the sequence
0.9, 0.99, 0.999, 0.9999, 0.99999, . . . .
6.4 Limsup, Liminf, and Limit Points
121
The number 0.8 is 0.1-adherent to this sequence, since 0.8 is 0.1-close to 0.9, which
is a member of this sequence. However, it is not continually 0.1-adherent to this
sequence, since once one discards the first element of this sequence there is no
member of the sequence to be 0.1-close to. In particular, 0.8 is not a limit point of
this sequence. On the other hand, the number 1 is 0.1-adherent to this sequence, and
in fact is continually 0.1-adherent to this sequence, since no matter how many initial
members of the sequence one discards, there is still something for 1 to be 0.1-close
to. In fact, it is continually \varepsilon-adherent for every \varepsilon, and is hence a limit point of this
sequence.
Example 6.4.4 Now consider the sequence
1.1, −1.01, 1.001, −1.0001, 1.00001, . . . .
The number 1 is 0.1-adherent to this sequence; in fact it is continually 0.1-adherent to
this sequence, because no matter how many elements of the sequence one discards,
there are some elements of the sequence that 1 is 0.1-close to. (As discussed earlier,
one does not need all the elements to be 0.1-close to 1, just some; thus 0.1-adherent
is weaker than 0.1-close, and continually 0.1-adherent is a different notion from
eventually 0.1-close.) In fact, for every \varepsilon > 0, the number 1 is continually \varepsilon-adherent
to this sequence and is thus a limit point of this sequence. Similarly, −1 is a limit
point of this sequence; however 0 (say) is not a limit point of this sequence, since it
is not continually 0.1-adherent to it.
Limits are of course a special case of limit points:
Proposition 6.4.5 (Limits are limit points). Let (an)\infty
n=m be a sequence which converges to a real number c. Then c is a limit point of (an)\infty
n=m, and in fact it is the only
limit point of (an)\infty
n=m.
Proof See Exercise 6.4.1.
□
Now we will look at two special types of limit points: the limit superior (lim sup)
and limit inferior (lim inf).

n=m is a
sequence. We define a new sequence (a+
N)\infty
N=m by the formula
a+
N := sup(an)\infty
n=N.
More informally, a+
N is the supremum of all the elements in the sequence from
aN onwards. We then define the limit superior of the sequence (an)\infty
n=m, denoted
lim supn\to\inftyan, by the formula
lim sup
n\to\infty
an := inf(a+
N)\infty
N=m.
Similarly, we can define

n=N

n=m, denoted lim infn\to\inftyan, by the

Then a+

(why?), and its infimum is 1. Hence the limit superior of this sequence is 1. Similarly,

Then a+

(why?) and so the limit superior is +\infty. Similarly, a−

2 , . . . is the sequence
−\infty, −\infty, −\infty, −\infty, . . .
and so the limit inferior is −\infty.
Example 6.4.9 Let a1, a2, a3, . . . denote the sequence
1, −1/2, 1/3, −1/4, 1/5, −1/6, . . .
Then a+
1 , a+
2 , . . . is the sequence
1, 1/3, 1/3, 1/5, 1/5, 1/7, . . .
6.4 Limsup, Liminf, and Limit Points
123
which has an infimum of 0 (why?), so the limit superior is 0. Similarly, a−
1 , a−
2 , . . .
is the sequence
−1/2, −1/2, −1/4, −1/4, −1/6, −1/6, . . .
which has a supremum of 0. So the limit inferior is also 0.
Example 6.4.10 Let a1, a2, a3, . . . denote the sequence
1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, . . .
Then a+

+\infty, +\infty, +\infty, . . .
so the limit superior is +\infty. Similarly, a−

which has a supremum of +\infty. So the limit inferior is also +\infty.
Remark 6.4.11 Some authors use the notation limn\to\inftyan instead of lim supn\to\inftyan,
and limn\to\inftyan instead of lim infn\to\inftyan. Note that the starting index m of the
sequence is irrelevant (see Exercise 6.4.2).
Returning to the piston analogy, imagine a piston at +\inftymoving leftward until
it is stopped by the presence of the sequence a1, a2, . . .. The place it will stop is the
supremum of a1, a2, a3, . . ., which in our new notation is a+
1 . Now let us remove the
first element a1 from the sequence; this may cause our piston to slip leftward, to a
new point a+
2 (though in many cases the piston will not move and a+
2 will just be
the same as a+
1 ). Then we remove the second element a2, causing the piston to slip a
little more. If we keep doing this the piston will keep slipping, but there will be some
point where it cannot go any further, and this is the limit superior of the sequence. A
similar analogy can describe the limit inferior of the sequence.
We now describe some basic properties of limit superior and limit inferior.
Proposition 6.4.12 Let (an)\infty
n=m be a sequence of real numbers, let L+ be the limit

(In other words, for every x < L+, the elements of the sequence (an)\infty
n=m exceed
x infinitely often.) Similarly, for every y > L−and every N \geqm, there exists an

n=m. Similarly, if L−is finite, then it

N,

n=m, and L−is the smallest limit point (provided that L+ and L−are
finite). Proposition 6.4.12 (f) then says that if L+ and L−coincide (so there is only
one limit point) and are finite, then the sequence in fact converges. This gives a way
to test if a sequence converges: compute its limit superior and limit inferior, and see
if they are equal.
We now give a basic comparison property of limit superior and limit inferior.
Lemma 6.4.13 (Comparison principle). Suppose that (an)\infty
n=m and (bn)\infty
n=m are two

Proof See Exercise 6.4.4.
□
Corollary 6.4.14 (Squeeze test). Let (an)\infty
n=m, (bn)\infty
n=m, and (cn)\infty
n=m be sequences
of real numbers such that
an \leqbn \leqcn
for all n \geqm. Suppose also that (an)\infty
n=m and (cn)\infty
n=m both converge to the same
limit L. Then (bn)\infty
n=m is also convergent to L.
6.4 Limsup, Liminf, and Limit Points
125
Proof See Exercise 6.4.5.
□
Example 6.4.15 We already know (see Proposition 6.1.11) that limn\to\infty1/n = 0.
By the limit laws (Theorem 6.1.19), this also implies that limn\to\infty2/n = 0 and
limn\to\infty−2/n = 0. The squeeze test then shows that any sequence (bn)\infty
n=1 for which
−2/n \leqbn \leq2/n for all n \geq1
is convergent to 0. For instance, we can use this to show that the sequence (−1)n/n +
1/n2 converges to zero, or that 2−n converges to zero. Note one can use induction to
show that 0 \leq2−n \leq1/n for all n \geq1.
Remark 6.4.16 The squeeze test, combined with the limit laws and the principle
that monotone bounded sequences always have limits, allows one to compute a large
number of limits. We give some examples in the next chapter.
One commonly used consequence of the squeeze test is
Corollary 6.4.17 (Zero test for sequences). Let (an)\infty
n=M be a sequence of real numbers. Then the limit limn\to\inftyan exists and is equal to zero if and only if the limit
limn\to\infty|an| exists and is equal to zero.
Proof See Exercise 6.4.7.
□
We close this section with the following improvement to Proposition 6.1.12.
Theorem 6.4.18 (Completeness of the reals). A sequence (an)\infty
n=1 of real numbers
is a Cauchy sequence if and only if it is convergent.
Remark 6.4.19 Note that while this is very similar in spirit to Proposition 6.1.15, it
is a bit more general, since Proposition 6.1.15 refers to Cauchy sequences of rationals
instead of real numbers.
Proof Proposition 6.1.12 already tells us that every convergent sequence is Cauchy,
so it suffices to show that every Cauchy sequence is convergent.
Let (an)\infty
n=1 be a Cauchy sequence. We know from Lemma 5.1.15 (or more
precisely, from the extension of this lemma to the real numbers, which is proven
in exactly the same fashion) that the sequence (an)\infty
n=1 is bounded; by Lemma
6.4.13 (or Proposition 6.4.12(c)) this implies that L−:= lim infn\to\inftyan and L+ :=
lim supn\to\inftyan of the sequence are both finite. To show that the sequence converges,
it will suffice by Proposition 6.4.12(f) to show that L−= L+.
Now let \varepsilon > 0 be any real number. Since (an)\infty
n=1 is a Cauchy sequence, it must
be eventually \varepsilon-steady, so in particular there exists an N \geq1 such that the sequence
(an)\infty
n=N is \varepsilon-steady. In particular, we have aN −\varepsilon \leqan \leqaN + \varepsilon for all n \geqN. By
Proposition 6.3.6 (or Lemma 6.4.13) this implies that
aN −\varepsilon \leqinf(an)\infty
n=N \leqsup(an)\infty
n=N \leqaN + \varepsilon

and hence by the definition of L−and L+ (and Proposition 6.3.6 again)
aN −\varepsilon \leqL−\leqL+ \leqaN + \varepsilon.
Thus we have
0 \leqL+ −L−\leq2\varepsilon.
But this is true for all \varepsilon > 0, and L+ and L−do not depend on \varepsilon; so we must therefore
have L+ = L−. (If L+ > L−then we could set \varepsilon := (L+ −L−)/3 and obtain a
contradiction.) By Proposition 6.4.12(f) we thus see that the sequence converges. □
Remark 6.4.20 In the language of metric spaces (see Chap. 1 of Analysis II), Theorem 6.4.18 asserts that the real numbers are a complete metric space—that they do
not contain “holes” the same way the rationals do. (Certainly the rationals have lots
of Cauchy sequences which do not converge to other rationals; take for instance the
sequence 1, 1.4, 1.41, 1.414, 1.4142, . . . which converges to the irrational
√
2.) This
property is closely related to the least upper bound property (Theorem 5.5.9), and
is one of the principal characteristics which make the real numbers superior to the
rational numbers for the purposes of doing analysis (taking limits, taking derivatives
and integrals, finding zeroes of functions, that kind of thing), as we shall see in later
chapters.

n=1 and (bn)\infty
n=1 such that an < bn
for all n \geq1, but that sup(an)\infty
n=1 ̸< sup(bn)\infty
n=1. Explain why this does not contradict Lemma
6.4.13.

of this corollary by some other number?

n=M of real numbers has +\inftyas a limit point iff
it has no finite upper bound, and that it has −\inftyas a limit point iff it has no finite lower bound.
With this definition, show that lim supn\to\inftyan is a limit point of (an)\infty
n=M, and furthermore that it is
larger than all the other limit points of (an)\infty
n=M; in other words, the limit superior is the largest limit

(One can use Proposition 6.4.12 in the course of the proof.)

# 6.4 上确界、下确界与极限点

考虑序列
$$1.1,\ -1.01,\ 1.001,\ -1.0001,\ 1.00001,\ \ldots$$

如果画出这个序列的图像，那么（当然是非正式地）可以看出该序列不收敛；有一半的时间序列接近 $1$，有一半的时间序列接近 $-1$，但它既不收敛到 $1$ 也不收敛到 $-1$；例如，它永远不会最终与 $1$ 的距离达到 $1/2$ 以内，也永远不会最终与 $-1$ 的距离达到 $1/2$ 以内。然而，尽管 $-1$ 和 $+1$ 并不完全是该序列的极限，但在某种模糊的意义上，它们"想要"成为极限。为了精确地表述这个概念，我们引入极限点的概念。

**定义 6.4.1（极限点）** 设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是一个实数序列，$x$ 是一个实数，$\varepsilon > 0$ 是一个实数。我们称 $x$ **$\varepsilon$-附着**于序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$，当且仅当存在 $n \geq m$ 使得 $a_n$ 与 $x$ 的距离在 $\varepsilon$ 以内。我们称 $x$ **持续 $\varepsilon$-附着**于序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$，当且仅当对于每个 $N \geq m$，$x$ 都 $\varepsilon$-附着于 $(a_n)_{n=N}^{\infty}$。我们称 $x$ 是序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的**极限点**或**附着点**，当且仅当对于每个 $\varepsilon > 0$，$x$ 都持续 $\varepsilon$-附着于 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$。

**注 6.4.2** 动词"to adhere"的意思与"to stick to"（粘贴、附着）大致相同；因此有了"adherent"（附着）这个术语。

展开所有定义，我们看到 $x$ 是 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的极限点，当且仅当对于每个 $\varepsilon > 0$ 和每个 $N \geq m$，存在 $n \geq N$ 使得 $|a_n - x| \leq \varepsilon$。（为什么这与原定义等价？）注意序列与 $L$ **$\varepsilon$-接近**（这意味着序列的所有元素都与 $L$ 的距离在 $\varepsilon$ 以内）和 $L$ **$\varepsilon$-附着**于该序列（只需要序列的一个元素与 $L$ 的距离在 $\varepsilon$ 以内）之间的区别。此外，对于 $L$ 持续 $\varepsilon$-附着于 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$，它必须对所有 $N \geq m$，$\varepsilon$-附着于 $(a_n)_{n=N}^{\infty}$，而对于 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 最终 $\varepsilon$-接近 $L$，我们只需要对某个 $N \geq m$，$(a_n)_{n=N}^{\infty}$ 与 $L$ $\varepsilon$-接近。

因此，极限与极限点在量词上存在一些微妙的区别。

注意极限点只对有限的实数定义。也可以严格地定义 $+\infty$ 或 $-\infty$ 作为极限点的概念；参见习题 6.4.8。

**例 6.4.3** 设 $(a_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 表示序列
$$0.9,\ 0.99,\ 0.999,\ 0.9999,\ 0.99999,\ \ldots$$

数 $0.8$ 对该序列是 $0.1$-附着的，因为 $0.8$ 与 $0.9$ 的距离在 $0.1$ 以内，而 $0.9$ 是该序列的一个元素。然而，它对该序列不是持续 $0.1$-附着的，因为一旦丢弃该序列的第一个元素后，就没有该序列的元素与 $0.8$ 的距离在 $0.1$ 以内了。特别地，$0.8$ 不是该序列的极限点。另一方面，数 $1$ 对该序列是 $0.1$-附着的，实际上对该序列是持续 $0.1$-附着的，因为无论丢弃该序列的前多少个元素，$1$ 仍然有元素与其距离在 $0.1$ 以内。事实上，对于每个 $\varepsilon > 0$，$1$ 都是持续 $\varepsilon$-附着的，因此是该序列的一个极限点。

**例 6.4.4** 现在考虑序列
$$1.1,\ -1.01,\ 1.001,\ -1.0001,\ 1.00001,\ \ldots$$

数 $1$ 对该序列是 $0.1$-附着的；实际上它对该序列是持续 $0.1$-附着的，因为无论丢弃该序列的多少个元素，该序列中总有一些元素与 $1$ 的距离在 $0.1$ 以内。（如前所述，不需要所有元素与 $1$ 的距离都在 $0.1$ 以内，只需要一些；因此 $0.1$-附着比 $0.1$-接近弱，持续 $0.1$-附着是不同于最终 $0.1$-接近的概念。）事实上，对于每个 $\varepsilon > 0$，数 $1$ 都对该序列持续 $\varepsilon$-附着，因此是该序列的极限点。类似地，$-1$ 也是该序列的极限点；然而 $0$（比如）不是该序列的极限点，因为它对该序列不是持续 $0.1$-附着的。

极限当然是极限点的特例：

**命题 6.4.5（极限是极限点）** 设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是一个收敛于实数 $c$ 的序列。则 $c$ 是 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的极限点，事实上它是 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 唯一的极限点。

证明 参见习题 6.4.1。

□

现在我们来看两种特殊的极限点：上极限（lim sup）和下极限（lim inf）。

**定义 6.4.6（上极限与下极限）** 设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是一个序列。我们用公式
$$a_N^+ := \sup(a_n)_{n=N}^{\infty}$$
定义一个新序列 $(a_N^+)_{N=m}^{\infty}$。更非正式地说，$a_N^+$ 是从 $a_N$ 开始序列中所有元素的上确界。然后我们用公式
$$\limsup_{n\to\infty} a_n := \inf(a_N^+)_{N=m}^{\infty}$$
定义序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的**上极限**，记作 $\limsup_{n\to\infty} a_n$。

类似地，我们定义
$$a_N^- := \inf(a_n)_{n=N}^{\infty}$$
并用公式
$$\liminf_{n\to\infty} a_n := \sup(a_N^-)_{N=m}^{\infty}$$
定义序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的**下极限**，记作 $\liminf_{n\to\infty} a_n$。

**例 6.4.7** 设 $a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots$ 表示序列
$$1.1,\ -1.01,\ 1.001,\ -1.0001,\ 1.00001,\ \ldots$$

则 $a_1^+, a_2^+, a_3^+, \ldots$ 是序列
$$1.1,\ 1.001,\ 1.001,\ 1.00001,\ 1.00001,\ \ldots$$
（为什么？），其下确界是 $1$。因此该序列的上极限是 $1$。类似地，$a_1^-, a_2^-, a_3^-, \ldots$ 是序列
$$-1.01,\ -1.01,\ -1.0001,\ -1.0001,\ -1.000001,\ \ldots$$
（为什么？），其上确界是 $-1$。因此该序列的下极限是 $-1$。应该将该序列的上极限和下极限与其上确界和下确界进行比较，它们分别是 $1.1$ 和 $-1.01$。

**例 6.4.8** 设 $a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots$ 表示序列
$$1,\ -2,\ 3,\ -4,\ 5,\ -6,\ 7,\ -8,\ \ldots$$

则 $a_1^+, a_2^+, \ldots$ 是序列
$$+\infty,\ +\infty,\ +\infty,\ +\infty,\ \ldots$$
（为什么？），因此上极限是 $+\infty$。类似地，$a_1^-, a_2^-, \ldots$ 是序列
$$-\infty,\ -\infty,\ -\infty,\ -\infty,\ \ldots$$
因此下极限是 $-\infty$。

**例 6.4.9** 设 $a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots$ 表示序列
$$1,\ -1/2,\ 1/3,\ -1/4,\ 1/5,\ -1/6,\ \ldots$$

则 $a_1^+, a_2^+, \ldots$ 是序列
$$1,\ 1/3,\ 1/3,\ 1/5,\ 1/5,\ 1/7,\ \ldots$$
其下确界是 $0$（为什么？），因此上极限是 $0$。类似地，$a_1^-, a_2^-, \ldots$ 是序列
$$-1/2,\ -1/2,\ -1/4,\ -1/4,\ -1/6,\ -1/6,\ \ldots$$
其上确界是 $0$。因此下极限也是 $0$。

**例 6.4.10** 设 $a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots$ 表示序列
$$1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6,\ \ldots$$

则 $a_1^+, a_2^+, \ldots$ 是序列
$$+\infty,\ +\infty,\ +\infty,\ \ldots$$
因此上极限是 $+\infty$。类似地，$a_1^-, a_2^-, \ldots$ 是序列
$$1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ \ldots$$
其上确界是 $+\infty$。因此下极限也是 $+\infty$。

**注 6.4.11** 有些作者用记号 $\underline{\lim}_{n\to\infty} a_n$ 代替 $\limsup_{n\to\infty} a_n$，用记号 $\underline{\lim}_{n\to\infty} a_n$ 代替 $\liminf_{n\to\infty} a_n$。注意序列的起始下标 $m$ 是无关的（参见习题 6.4.2）。

回到活塞的类比，想象一个在 $+\infty$ 处的活塞向左移动，直到被序列 $a_1, a_2, \ldots$ 的存在所阻挡。它停止的位置是 $a_1, a_2, a_3, \ldots$ 的上确界，用我们的新记号表示就是 $a_1^+$。现在我们从序列中移除第一个元素 $a_1$；这可能导致我们的活塞向左滑动到一个新的位置 $a_2^+$（不过在很多情况下活塞不会移动，$a_2^+$ 与 $a_1^+$ 相同）。然后我们移除第二个元素 $a_2$，导致活塞再滑动一点。如果我们一直这样做，活塞会不断滑动，但最终会到达某个无法继续向前的点，这就是该序列的上极限。一个类似的类比可以描述该序列的下极限。

现在我们描述上极限和下极限的一些基本性质。

**命题 6.4.12** 设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是一个实数序列，设 $L^+$ 是该序列的上极限，$L^-$ 是该序列的下极限（因此 $L^+$ 和 $L^-$ 都是扩展实数）。

(a) 对于每个 $x > L^+$，存在 $N \geq m$ 使得对于所有 $n \geq N$，$a_n < x$。（换句话说，对于每个 $x > L^+$，序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的元素最终都小于 $x$。）类似地，对于每个 $y < L^-$，存在 $N \geq m$ 使得对于所有 $n \geq N$，$a_n > y$。

(b) 对于每个 $x < L^+$ 和每个 $N \geq m$，存在 $n \geq N$ 使得 $a_n > x$。（换句话说，对于每个 $x < L^+$，序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的元素无限次超过 $x$。）类似地，对于每个 $y > L^-$ 和每个 $N \geq m$，存在 $n \geq N$ 使得 $a_n < y$。

(c) 我们有 $\inf(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty} \leq L^- \leq L^+ \leq \sup(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$。

(d) 如果 $c$ 是 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的任意极限点，则我们有 $L^- \leq c \leq L^+$。

(e) 如果 $L^+$ 是有限的，则它是 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的一个极限点。类似地，如果 $L^-$ 是有限的，则它是 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的一个极限点。

(f) 设 $c$ 是一个实数。如果 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛于 $c$，则必有 $L^+ = L^- = c$。反之，如果 $L^+ = L^- = c$，则 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 收敛于 $c$。

证明 我们将证明 (a) 和 (b)，其余部分留给习题。

首先假设 $x > L^+$。则由 $L^+$ 的定义，我们有 $x > \inf(a_N^+)_{N=m}^{\infty}$。由命题 6.3.6，必存在整数 $N \geq m$ 使得 $x > a_N^+$。由 $a_N^+$ 的定义，这意味着 $x > \sup(a_n)_{n=N}^{\infty}$。再次由命题 6.3.6，我们对于所有 $n \geq N$ 有 $x > a_n$，正是所证。这证明了 (a) 的第一部分；(a) 的第二部分可类似证明。

现在证明 (b)。假设 $x < L^+$。则我们有 $x < \inf(a_N^+)_{N=m}^{\infty}$。如果我们固定任意 $N \geq m$，则由命题 6.3.6，我们有 $x < a_N^+$。由 $a_N^+$ 的定义，这意味着 $x < \sup(a_n)_{n=N}^{\infty}$。再次由命题 6.3.6，必存在 $n \geq N$ 使得 $a_n > x$，正是所证。这证明了 (b) 的第一部分，(b) 的第二部分可类似证明。

(c)、(d)、(e)、(f) 的证明留给习题 6.4.3。

□

命题 6.4.12 的 (d) 和 (e) 部分特别说明，$L^+$ 是 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的最大极限点，$L^-$ 是 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的最小极限点（前提是 $L^+$ 和 $L^-$ 是有限的）。命题 6.4.12 (f) 则说明，如果 $L^+$ 和 $L^-$ 重合（即只有一个极限点）且是有限的，则该序列实际上收敛。这给出了一种检验序列是否收敛的方法：计算它的上极限和下极限，看它们是否相等。

现在我们给出上极限和下极限的一个基本比较性质。

**引理 6.4.13（比较原理）** 设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 和 $(b_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是两个实数序列，使得对于所有 $n \geq m$，$a_n \leq b_n$。则有不等式
$$\sup(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty} \leq \sup(b_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$$
$$\inf(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty} \leq \inf(b_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$$
$$\limsup_{n\to\infty} a_n \leq \limsup_{n\to\infty} b_n$$
$$\liminf_{n\to\infty} a_n \leq \liminf_{n\to\infty} b_n$$

证明 参见习题 6.4.4。

□

**推论 6.4.14（夹逼定理）** 设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$、$(b_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 和 $(c_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 是实数序列，满足
$$a_n \leq b_n \leq c_n$$
对所有 $n \geq m$ 成立。假设 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 和 $(c_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 都收敛于同一极限 $L$。则 $(b_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 也收敛于 $L$。

证明 参见习题 6.4.5。

□

**例 6.4.15** 我们已知（见命题 6.1.11）$\lim_{n\to\infty} 1/n = 0$。由极限定律（定理 6.1.19），这也意味着 $\lim_{n\to\infty} 2/n = 0$ 和 $\lim_{n\to\infty} -2/n = 0$。夹逼定理则表明，任何满足
$$-2/n \leq b_n \leq 2/n$$
对所有 $n \geq 1$ 成立的序列 $(b_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}$ 都收敛于 $0$。例如，我们可以利用它证明序列 $(-1)^n/n + 1/n^2$ 收敛于零，或者 $2^{-n}$ 收敛于零。注意可以用数学归纳法证明对所有 $n \geq

Exercise 6.4.10 Let (an)\infty

6.5
Some Standard Limits
Armed now with the limit laws and the squeeze test, we can now compute a large
number of limits.
Aparticularlysimplelimitisthatoftheconstantsequencec, c, c, c, . . .;weclearly
have
lim
n\to\inftyc = c
for any constant c (why?).
Also, in Proposition 6.1.11, we proved that limn\to\infty1/n = 0. This now implies
Corollary 6.5.1 We have limn\to\infty1/n1/k = 0 for every integer k \geq1.
Proof From Lemma 5.6.6 we know that 1/n1/k is a decreasing function of n, while
being bounded below by 0. By Proposition 6.3.8 (for decreasing sequences instead
of increasing sequences) we thus know that this sequence converges to some limit
L \geq0:
L = lim
n\to\infty1/n1/k.
Raising this to the kth power and using the limit laws (or more precisely, Theorem
6.1.19(b) and induction), we obtain
Lk = lim
n\to\infty1/n.
By Proposition 6.1.11 we thus have Lk = 0; but this means that L cannot be positive
(else Lk would be positive), so L = 0, and we are done.
□
Some other basic limits:
Lemma 6.5.2 Let x be a real number. Then the limit limn\to\inftyxn exists and is equal
to zero when |x| < 1, exists and is equal to 1 when x = 1, and diverges when x = −1
or when |x| > 1.
Proof See Exercise 6.5.2.
□
Lemma 6.5.3 For any x > 0, we have limn\to\inftyx1/n = 1.
Proof See Exercise 6.5.3.
□
128
6
Limits of Sequences
We will derive a few more standard limits later on, once we develop the root and
ratio tests for series and for sequences.
— Exercises —
Exercise 6.5.1 Show that limn\to\infty1/nq = 0 for any rational q > 0. (Hint: use Corollary 6.5.1 and
the limit laws, Theorem 6.1.19.) Conclude that the limit limn\to\inftynq does not exist. (Hint: argue by
contradiction using Theorem 6.1.19(e).)
Exercise 6.5.2 Prove Lemma 6.5.2. (Hint: use Proposition 6.3.10, Exercise 6.3.4, and the squeeze
test.)
Exercise 6.5.3 Prove Lemma 6.5.3. (Hint: you may need to treat the cases x \geq1 and x < 1 separately. You might wish to first use Lemma 6.5.2 to prove the preliminary result that for every \varepsilon > 0
and every real number M > 0, there exists an n such that M1/n \leq1 + \varepsilon.)
6.6
Subsequences
This chapter has been devoted to the study of sequences (an)\infty
n=m of real numbers,
and their limits. Some sequences were convergent to a single limit, while others had
multiple limit points. For instance, the sequence
1.1, 0.1, 1.01, 0.01, 1.001, 0.001, 1.0001, . . .
has two limit points at 0 and 1 (which are incidentally also the lim inf and lim sup
respectively), but is not actually convergent (since the lim sup and lim inf are not
equal). However, while this sequence is not convergent, it does appear to contain
convergent components; it seems to be a mixture of two convergent subsequences,
namely
1.1, 1.01, 1.001, . . .
and
0.1, 0.01, 0.001, . . . .
To make this notion more precise, we need a notion of subsequence.
Definition 6.6.1 (Subsequences). Let (an)\infty
n=0 and (bn)\infty
n=0 be sequences of real numbers. We say that (bn)\infty
n=0 is a subsequence of (an)\infty
n=0 iff there exists a function
f : N \toN which is strictly increasing (i.e., f (n + 1) > f (n) for all n \inN) such
that
bn = a f (n) for all n \inN.
More generally, we say that (bn)\infty
n=m′ is a subsequence of (an)\infty
n=m if there exists a
strictly increasing function f : {n \inN : n \geqm′} \to{n \inN : n \geqm} such that bn =
a f (n) for all n \inN with n \geqm′.
sections.content_zh:
6.5 一些标准极限

运用极限定律和夹逼定理，我们现在可以计算大量的极限。

一个特别简单的极限是常数序列 $c, c, c, c, \dots$；显然我们有

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} c = c$$

对任意常数 $c$（为什么？）。

此外，在命题 6.1.11 中，我们证明了 $\lim_{n \to \infty} 1/n = 0$。由此可得

推论 6.5.1 对每个整数 $k \geq 1$，我们有 $\lim_{n \to \infty} 1/n^{1/k} = 0$。

**证明** 由引理 5.6.6 可知，$1/n^{1/k}$ 是关于 $n$ 的递减函数，同时下界为 $0$。由命题 6.3.8（适用于递减序列而非递增序列），我们因此知道该序列收敛于某个极限 $L \geq 0$：

$$L = \lim_{n \to \infty} 1/n^{1/k}.$$

对该式取 $k$ 次方，并利用极限定律（或更精确地说，定理 6.1.19(b) 与归纳法），我们得到

$$L^k = \lim_{n \to \infty} 1/n.$$

由命题 6.1.11 可得 $L^k = 0$；但这意味着 $L$ 不可能为正（否则 $L^k$ 将为正），所以 $L = 0$，证毕。

$\square$

其他一些基本极限：

**引理 6.5.2** 设 $x$ 为实数。那么极限 $\lim_{n \to \infty} x^n$ 存在且等于 $0$ 当且仅当 $|x| < 1$；存在且等于 $1$ 当 $x = 1$；而当 $x = -1$ 或 $|x| > 1$ 时发散。

**证明** 见练习 6.5.2。

$\square$

**引理 6.5.3** 对任意 $x > 0$，我们有 $\lim_{n \to \infty} x^{1/n} = 1$。

**证明** 见练习 6.5.3。

$\square$

我们将在后面推导更多的标准极限，届时会发展出级数的根判别法和比值判别法。

---

**—— 练习 ——**

**练习 6.5.1** 证明对任意有理数 $q > 0$，有 $\lim_{n \to \infty} 1/n^q = 0$。（提示：利用推论 6.5.1 和极限定律，定理 6.1.19。）由此推出极限 $\lim_{n \to \infty} n^q$ 不存在。（提示：利用定理 6.1.19(e) 用反证法证明。）

**练习 6.5.2** 证明引理 6.5.2。（提示：利用命题 6.3.10、练习 6.3.4 和夹逼定理。）

**练习 6.5.3** 证明引理 6.5.3。（提示：你可能需要分别处理 $x \geq 1$ 和 $x < 1$ 的情形。你可能想先用引理 6.5.2 证明一个初步结果：对每个 $\varepsilon > 0$ 和每个实数 $M > 0$，存在 $n$ 使得 $M^{1/n} \leq 1 + \varepsilon$。）

---

6.6 子序列

本章致力于研究实数序列 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 及其极限。有些序列收敛于单一极限，而另一些则有多个极限点。例如，序列

$$1.1, 0.1, 1.01, 0.01, 1.001, 0.001, 1.0001, \dots$$

在 $0$ 和 $1$ 处有两个极限点（顺便说一下，这也是该序列的 $\liminf$ 和 $\limsup$），但实际上并不收敛（因为 $\limsup$ 和 $\liminf$ 不相等）。然而，虽然该序列不收敛，但它似乎包含收敛的"成分"；它似乎是两个收敛子序列的混合，即

$$1.1, 1.01, 1.001, \dots$$

和

$$0.1, 0.01, 0.001, \dots$$

为了更精确地描述这一概念，我们需要引入子序列的概念。

**定义 6.6.1（子序列）** 设 $(a_n)_{n=0}^{\infty}$ 和 $(b_n)_{n=0}^{\infty}$ 是实数序列。我们称 $(b_n)_{n=0}^{\infty}$ 是 $(a_n)_{n=0}^{\infty}$ 的子序列，当且仅当存在一个严格递增的函数 $f: \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}$（即对所有 $n \in \mathbb{N}$ 有 $f(n+1) > f(n)$），使得

$$b_n = a_{f(n)} \quad \text{对所有 } n \in \mathbb{N} \text{ 成立。}$$

更一般地，如果存在一个严格递增的函数 $f: \{n \in \mathbb{N} : n \geq m'\} \to \{n \in \mathbb{N} : n \geq m\}$ 使得对所有 $n \geq m'$ 有 $b_n = a_{f(n)}$，则我们称 $(b_n)_{n=m'}^{\infty}$ 是 $(a_n)_{n=m}^{\infty}$ 的子序列。
